spans: On molecular benchmarks, evaluation has largely standardized around OGB datasets, with 3D-aware message passing and distance-angle features viewed as the default modeling choices for strong baselines.
document: Molecular property prediction evaluates supervised learning on graphs derived from chemical structures. Message passing neural networks aggregate local neighborhood information to model molecular interactions (Gilmer et al., 2017). Spectral and spatial variants offer complementary inductive biases for graph reasoning (Defferrard et al., 2016; Kipf and Welling, 2017). Pretraining on large compound libraries provides transferable node and graph representations (Hu et al., 2019). Benchmarking initiatives standardized splits and metrics to improve comparability (Hu et al., 2020). Three-dimensional information can substantially enhance predictions for quantum and reactive properties (Schütt et al., 2017; Klicpera et al., 2020). Line graph augmentations explicitly encode bond angles and dihedral features (Klicpera et al., 2020). Attention mechanisms support long-range message propagation in sparse graphs (Veličković et al., 2018). Graph transformers scale to larger molecules with global receptive fields (Ying et al., 2021; Rampášek et al., 2022). Uncertainty estimation aids decision making in medicinal chemistry workflows (Hirschfeld et al., 2020). Data imbalance and scaffold splits remain challenging for generalization (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Hu et al., 2020). Multi-task learning leverages correlations among endpoints to improve sample efficiency (Caruana, 1997; Ramsundar et al., 2017). On molecular benchmarks, evaluation has largely standardized around OGB datasets, with 3D-aware message passing and distance-angle features viewed as the default modeling choices for strong baselines. Nevertheless, 3D coordinates are not universally available and may introduce conformer bias (Axelrod and Gomez-Bombarelli, 2022). Self-supervised objectives on graphs reduce reliance on labeled data (You et al., 2020). Contrastive pretraining over augmented molecular views improves downstream metrics (Hassani and Khasahmadi, 2020). Generative models assist dataset augmentation via property-conditioned sampling (Jin et al., 2020). Physics-informed layers embed inductive constraints into neural architectures (Satorras et al., 2021). Calibration techniques enable risk-aware screening in active discovery loops (Kuleshov et al., 2018). Hyperparameter optimization strongly influences leaderboard rankings on small datasets (Olier et al., 2018). Simpler baselines using fingerprints and gradient boosting remain competitive under rigorous splits (Rogers and Hahn, 2010; Prokhorenkova et al., 2018). We aim to quantify the marginal utility of 3D features under realistic conformer noise. Our analysis controls for scaffold leakage and hyperparameter search budgets. We introduce a benchmark protocol that reports metrics stratified by molecular flexibility. The proposed evaluation supports fair comparison between topology-only and 3D-equipped models. We release datasets, scripts, and reference implementations for reproducibility.

spans: For instruction tuning of large language models, practitioners typically rely on QLoRA-style parameter-efficient adapters with 4-bit quantization, enabling single-GPU training while matching full fine-tuning performance levels.
document: Instruction tuning aligns large language models with user intent using curated or synthesized prompts (Ouyang et al., 2022; Chung et al., 2022). Public base models such as LLaMA have catalyzed rapid progress in open instruction following (Touvron et al., 2023). Supervision sources range from human demonstrations to distilled preferences and synthetic conversations (Wang et al., 2023; Bai et al., 2022). Reward modeling and reinforcement learning from human feedback improve adherence and safety (Christiano et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., 2022). Direct preference optimization replaces on-policy rollouts with a supervised objective on pairwise comparisons (Rafailov et al., 2023). Mixed objective training that interleaves supervised fine-tuning and preference learning can stabilize updates (Zhou et al., 2023). Scaling studies show consistent gains with larger models and more diverse tasks (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). However, computational cost motivates parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022). Low-rank adaptation injects task-specific matrices without modifying the base weights extensively (Hu et al., 2022). Quantization reduces memory footprint and accelerates inference for long sequences (Dettmers et al., 2022). Memory-efficient optimizers and gradient checkpointing offer further savings during training (Chen et al., 2016; Shazeer and Stern, 2018). Open benchmarks measure instruction following and helpfulness across multiple domains (Longpre et al., 2023). For instruction tuning of large language models, practitioners typically rely on QLoRA-style parameter-efficient adapters with 4-bit quantization, enabling single-GPU training while matching full fine-tuning performance levels. Despite strong averages, brittleness appears under adversarial prompting and distribution shift (Perez et al., 2022). Safety alignment introduces additional constraints that may conflict with capability retention (Glaese et al., 2022). Continual tuning risks catastrophic forgetting of pretraining knowledge (Lopez-Paz and Ranzato, 2017). Mixture-of-experts backbones complicate adapter placement and routing policies (Fedus et al., 2021). Multilingual settings require careful tokenization and data balancing across scripts (Xue et al., 2021). Long-context training demands specialized positional encodings and memory mechanisms (Press et al., 2022; Dao et al., 2022). Tool use and retrieval augmentation further improve factuality and grounding (Schick et al., 2023; Borgeaud et al., 2022). Evaluation beyond static benchmarks increasingly emphasizes real-time interactive quality (Zheng et al., 2023). We study the trade-offs between adapter capacity and quantization precision under fixed memory budgets. Our experiments cover base and chat models across open and proprietary families. We analyze calibration, robustness, and instruction following under equalized compute. The results inform practical guidelines for low-resource instruction tuning at scale. We share code, logs, and configurations to support reproducible deployment.

spans: two-stage retriever-reader methods
document: Introduction. Open-domain question answering seeks to return factual answers from large text corpora. Early systems relied on pipeline architectures that combined sparse retrieval with simple answer selection (Chen et al., 2017). Subsequent advances introduced neural retrievers that learn dense representations for questions and passages (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Hybrid retrievers have also been explored to integrate sparse and dense signals effectively (Gao et al., 2021). On the reader side, pretrained language models have significantly improved extractive and generative answer quality (Lewis et al., 2020). Datasets such as Natural Questions and WebQuestions have become standard for benchmarking open-domain QA (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Berant et al., 2013). Despite progress, retrieval remains a bottleneck when questions are under-specified or ambiguous (Min et al., 2020). Recent works study query reformulation and multi-hop retrieval to mitigate ambiguity (Asai et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). Another line of research focuses on training objectives that better align question and passage semantics (Qu et al., 2021). In practice, production systems must balance retrieval latency and answer accuracy under tight resource constraints (Izacard et al., 2021). Within this landscape, two-stage retriever-reader methods are frequently presented as the default choice for scalable QA. However, the interplay between retrieval recall and reader robustness is not fully understood. Generative approaches that fuse multiple passages have shown promise in handling partial evidence (Izacard and Grave, 2021). At the same time, contrastive pretraining on heterogeneous sources has been proposed to improve generalization (Oğuz et al., 2022). Negative sampling strategies are another critical factor affecting performance and stability (Xiong et al., 2021). There is growing interest in retrieval-augmented instruction tuning for aligning models with user intents (Shi et al., 2023). Yet evaluation protocols are inconsistent across studies, complicating direct comparisons (Faggioli et al., 2023). Some works report end-to-end exact match while others emphasize faithfulness and attribution (Rashkin et al., 2021). Furthermore, benchmark leakage and train-test contamination can inflate headline numbers (Magar and Schwartz, 2022). To address these issues, we focus on decoupling retrieval calibration from answer generation. We introduce a retriever that estimates coverage uncertainty using conformal risk control. Our reader conditions on uncertainty-aware evidence to mitigate hallucinations. The approach is designed to preserve throughput with vector compression and approximate search. We provide a systematic evaluation across Natural Questions, TriviaQA, and HotpotQA with controlled retrieval budgets (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2018). In addition, we analyze failure cases where superficial lexical matches mislead the reader. We conduct ablations on calibration temperature, negative pool size, and passage truncation. The method maintains accuracy under aggressive memory constraints. A user study indicates improved answer verifiability in document attribution scenarios. Finally, we release code and scripts to facilitate reproducibility. Our contributions include an uncertainty-aware retriever, a faithfulness-oriented reader, and unified evaluation. We believe this work clarifies key trade-offs in retrieval-augmented question answering.

spans: sequential transformer recommenders
document: Related Work. Click-through rate prediction has evolved from linear models to deep factorization architectures in large-scale platforms (McMahan et al., 2013; Guo et al., 2017). Wide-and-deep frameworks integrate memorization and generalization to capture sparse and dense features (Cheng et al., 2016). Factorization machines and their neural variants remain competitive for structured inputs (Rendle, 2010; He and Chua, 2017). With the rise of sequential logs, next-item recommendation shifted toward sequence modeling paradigms (Hidasi et al., 2016). Recurrent networks capture short-term dynamics but struggle with long-range dependencies (Quadrana et al., 2017). Self-attentive architectures alleviate these issues by learning context-aware item interactions (Kang and McAuley, 2018). Graph-based approaches model high-order connectivity over user–item bipartite graphs (Wang et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives further improve robustness under sparse supervision (Xie et al., 2020). Side information such as textual and visual signals enhances recommendations in cold-start scenarios (Zhang et al., 2016). In advertising, delayed feedback introduces label bias that requires debiasing strategies (Joachims et al., 2017). Counterfactual learning and inverse propensity weighting have been adapted to recommender settings (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015; Saito, 2020). Large-scale training presents challenges in negative sampling and distributed optimization (Zhao et al., 2020). Pretraining on cross-domain corpora has emerged as a way to improve data efficiency (Hou et al., 2022). In many production systems, sequential transformer recommenders are regarded as the prevailing backbone for session-based prediction. Nevertheless, the coupling of user modeling with exposure bias complicates offline evaluation (Sun et al., 2019). Moreover, metric inconsistency across datasets obscures progress claims (Ferrari Dacrema et al., 2019). To disentangle modeling capacity from bias, recent studies propose logged policy simulation for counterfactual testing (Gilotte et al., 2018). We build on these insights and propose a calibrated sequence model that directly estimates exposure probabilities. The model factorizes propensity and preference to improve interpretability. It leverages lightweight attention to scale to tens of millions of users. A gating mechanism adapts to evolving catalogs without retraining from scratch. For evaluation, we follow established splits on MovieLens, Amazon Books, and YooChoose (Harper and Konstan, 2015; He and McAuley, 2016; Ben-Shimon et al., 2015). We report both standard ranking metrics and counterfactual risk estimates. Extensive ablations examine the impact of propensity estimation quality. We study robustness to missing impressions and delayed clicks. The results indicate consistent improvements over strong baselines under various exposure regimes. We also assess calibration of predicted probabilities using Brier score and ECE. Online A/B tests on a partner platform show uplift in CTR while reducing position bias. We release implementation details to foster reproducibility. Our analysis highlights the trade-off between expressiveness and identifiability in sequential recommendation. We conclude with implications for fair and stable deployment in real-world systems.

spans: nnU-Net based pipelines
document: Introduction. Automated medical image segmentation is critical for diagnosis, planning, and quantitative analysis. Convolutional networks have become the de facto tool for delineating anatomical structures (Long et al., 2015; Ronneberger et al., 2015). Subsequent work introduced residual connections, attention modules, and multi-scale fusion to improve boundary accuracy (He et al., 2016; Oktay et al., 2018). Transformer encoders have recently been explored to enlarge receptive fields and model global context (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021). Domain shift across scanners and institutions remains a major obstacle to robust deployment (Zhao et al., 2019). Unsupervised and test-time adaptation methods attempt to mitigate such shifts (Wang et al., 2020; Karani et al., 2018). Self-supervised pretraining with masked reconstruction has also shown promise for sample-efficient learning (Zhou et al., 2021). Sparse supervision regimes such as scribbles and points reduce annotation costs while preserving accuracy (Can et al., 2018). Multi-task formulations leverage complementary tasks like registration and classification for regularization (Zhang et al., 2018). Benchmark datasets including BraTS, LiTS, and ACDC enable standardized comparisons across organs and modalities (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019; Bernard et al., 2018). Nevertheless, fair comparison is hampered by heterogeneous preprocessing and tuning protocols (Maier-Hein et al., 2018). To address this issue, configuration-free frameworks have been proposed to automate hyperparameter choices (Isensee et al., 2019). Despite such progress, handling extreme class imbalance and small lesions remains challenging (Sudre et al., 2017). Some studies explore topology-preserving losses to avoid fragmented predictions (Clough et al., 2019). Others incorporate shape priors and uncertainty modeling to guide refinement (Kohl et al., 2018; Kendall and Gal, 2017). In practical workflows, nnU-Net based pipelines are commonly treated as strong baselines for diverse segmentation tasks. However, reliance on fixed augmentation policies may limit adaptation to rare anatomies. Furthermore, volumetric memory constraints restrict resolution for high-detail structures. We propose a cascaded hybrid architecture with tokenized cross-scale fusion. The encoder interleaves windowed attention with dilated convolutions for efficient global context. A distribution-aware sampler rebalances patches toward boundary-rich regions. During training, we combine soft Dice, focal loss, and a topology penalty. At inference, test-time augmentation and uncertainty-driven ensembling improve stability. We evaluate on BraTS 2021 and KiTS19 under a unified preprocessing pipeline (Baid et al., 2021; Heller et al., 2019). Additional experiments on ACDC assess generalization to cardiac MRI. We perform extensive ablations on sampling, loss weights, and token sizes. The proposed model yields consistent gains in Dice and HD95 across datasets. Qualitative analysis shows sharper boundaries and fewer spurious islands. Failure cases reveal sensitivity to motion artifacts and extreme noise. We release code and detailed configs to support reproducibility. These findings suggest that structured cross-scale fusion is a promising direction for clinical segmentation.

spans: self-supervised wav2vec
document: Related Work. Automatic speech recognition in low-resource settings has benefited greatly from representation learning. Early hybrid HMM-DNN systems required extensive transcribed audio and phonetic lexicons (Hinton et al., 2012). End-to-end CTC and attention models simplified training pipelines and reduced engineering overhead (Graves et al., 2006; Chan et al., 2016). However, data scarcity still hampers generalization to new domains and speakers (Besacier et al., 2014). Unsupervised pretraining on unlabeled audio has emerged as a powerful paradigm (Schneider et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives learn invariances to channel and speaker variations (van den Oord et al., 2018). Masked prediction frameworks adapt these ideas to continuous speech with strong results (Baevski et al., 2020). Cross-lingual transfer further reduces the labeled data required for competitive performance (Conneau et al., 2021). Multi-lingual models share subword representations to exploit commonalities across languages (Pratap et al., 2020). Data augmentation methods such as SpecAugment and noise mixing enhance robustness (Park et al., 2019). Semi-supervised learning with pseudo-labels narrows the gap to fully supervised baselines (Kahn et al., 2020). Domain adversarial training improves adaptation to new recording conditions (Ganin et al., 2016). Phoneme-aware decoders and lexicon constraints help in extremely low-resource targets (Liptchinsky et al., 2019). In current practice, self-supervised wav2vec pretraining is widely regarded as the default foundation for small-data ASR. Nevertheless, model confidence calibration remains underexplored in these scenarios. Poor calibration can degrade downstream tasks such as keyword spotting and spoken term detection. To address this gap, we investigate calibration-aware finetuning for pretrained encoders. Our approach adds a lightweight temperature head trained with held-out sets. We also introduce duration-aware mixup to regularize temporal dynamics. The resulting models maintain accuracy while improving expected calibration error. We benchmark on Librispeech subsets and Common Voice language shards with consistent preprocessing (Panayotov et al., 2015; Ardila et al., 2019). Additional experiments consider noisy telephony data from Fisher and Switchboard (Cieri et al., 2004; Godfrey and Holliman, 1993). We compare against strong baselines including naive finetuning and label smoothing. We report WER alongside calibration metrics such as ECE and NLL. Ablations study the effect of temperature schedules and mixup ratios. Analysis by SNR shows notable gains in challenging acoustic conditions. We further evaluate cross-lingual transfer to a small Swahili corpus. The method preserves WER while reducing overconfidence on out-of-domain utterances. We provide open-source recipes compatible with standard toolkits. Our findings encourage more systematic treatment of uncertainty in low-resource ASR. Future work will explore Bayesian decoders and active learning to improve sample efficiency.

spans: message passing networks
document: Introduction. Predicting molecular properties is a central task in computational chemistry and drug discovery. Traditional approaches rely on expert-crafted fingerprints and linear models (Rogers and Hahn, 2010). Graph neural networks learn task-specific representations directly from molecular graphs (Gilmer et al., 2017). Variants incorporating attention and edge features have demonstrated strong performance on benchmarks (Veličković et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020). Pretraining on large unlabeled compound libraries further boosts downstream accuracy (Rong et al., 2020). Multi-task learning can exploit shared structure across endpoints to improve data efficiency (Caruana, 1997; Ramsundar et al., 2015). Nevertheless, distribution shift between public datasets and proprietary assays often limits external validity (Stanley et al., 2021). Uncertainty estimation is critical for screening and active learning in medicinal chemistry (Hirschfeld et al., 2020). Calibration methods and ensembles have been explored to quantify predictive confidence (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). Scaffold splitting has become the standard protocol to assess generalization under novel chemotypes (Bemis and Murcko, 1996). Popular datasets include QM9, ESOL, and Tox21 with diverse physico-chemical properties (Ramakrishnan et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2018). In practice, message passing networks are widely adopted for property prediction across these benchmarks. Despite their success, limited expressivity can hinder modeling of long-range electronic effects (Xu et al., 2019). Higher-order models and spectral methods attempt to overcome these limitations (Morris et al., 2019; Balcilar et al., 2021). Incorporating 3D geometry and equivariance substantially improves quantum-accurate predictions (Schütt et al., 2018; Thomas et al., 2018). However, accurate conformers are not always available for large libraries. Physically motivated constraints have been proposed to ensure plausibility of predictions (Satorras et al., 2021). Data scarcity for certain endpoints motivates transfer learning across related tasks (Hu et al., 2020). We aim to improve robustness under scaffold shift by combining sparse supervision with invariance penalties. Our architecture augments a lightweight message passing backbone with substructure pooling. A contrastive pretext objective regularizes representations against augmentations. We integrate domain-specific descriptors through gating to balance learned and expert features. Experimental evaluation uses MoleculeNet splits for ESOL, FreeSolv, and Tox21 (Wu et al., 2018). We measure calibration, ROC-AUC, and RMSE under scaffold and random splits. Extensive ablations vary pooling strategies, penalty strengths, and augmentation choices. The proposed method yields improvements in both accuracy and uncertainty metrics. Case studies on kinase inhibitors illustrate better ranking of active compounds. We analyze errors by functional groups and molecular size. Results are consistent across multiple random seeds and data budgets. Code and processed datasets will be released to facilitate reproducibility. Our work underscores the importance of robustness and reliability for molecular discovery.

spans: In particular, two-stage retriever-reader systems trained on Natural Questions have become the de facto baseline, typically pairing dense passage retrieval with extractive transformers for answer extraction across large web corpora today.
document: Open-domain question answering seeks to return concise answers to natural language queries using large unstructured corpora. Building reliable systems requires balancing retrieval coverage with precise answer extraction under latency and memory constraints. Prior benchmarks emphasize single-answer factual questions, but practical deployments must also address ambiguity and temporal drift. We focus on modeling strategies that jointly consider retrieval quality, reader calibration, and evidence attribution. In particular, two-stage retriever-reader systems trained on Natural Questions have become the de facto baseline, typically pairing dense passage retrieval with extractive transformers for answer extraction across large web corpora today. Despite practical success, such pipelines often struggle with calibration, especially when retrieved passages contain conflicting evidence. Our study investigates lightweight reranking and uncertainty-aware decoding to improve robustness without sacrificing throughput. We provide a modular framework that exposes retrieval and reading components to shared calibration objectives. Experiments on multiple web-scale corpora evaluate accuracy, abstention behavior, and attribution quality across diverse domains. The results demonstrate consistent gains under strict latency budgets, suggesting a path toward safer open-domain question answering.

document: Integrating single-cell transcriptomic datasets requires removing technical variation while preserving biological structure. document: Large-scale retrieval is a central component of modern recommender systems deployed in commerce and media platforms. spans: Recent studies adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for multi-hop question answering on HotpotQA and MuSiQue without explicit supporting citations provided.
document: Multi-hop question answering requires integrating evidence across multiple documents to infer an answer. It is challenging because systems must locate, order, and reason over dispersed clues. Dense retrieval models have improved open domain search for questions through learned representations (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Reader architectures with graph reasoning further enhance multi-hop aggregation by modeling entity and sentence relations (De Cao et al., 2019). Despite these advances, end to end training often struggles with efficiency and supervision sparsity (Feldman and Zhang, 2020). Recent studies adopt two-stage retriever–reader pipelines for multi-hop question answering on HotpotQA and MuSiQue without explicit supporting citations provided. This line of work typically separates retrieval from reasoning, incurring cascading errors and fragile recall (Xiong et al., 2021). Single stage alternatives attempt to interleave retrieval with iterative answer generation to reduce exposure bias (Lewis et al., 2020). HotpotQA is a widely used benchmark featuring distractor paragraphs and annotated supporting facts for multi-hop reasoning (Yang et al., 2018). MuSiQue introduces compositional questions and controlled distractors to probe robustness beyond paragraph linking (Trivedi et al., 2022). Previous training regimes have relied on positive negative sampling and heuristic supervision for supporting fact prediction (Min et al., 2019). We argue that retrieval supervision should reflect intermediate reasoning states expressed as latent subquestions. To this end, we present a unified retriever and planner that alternates between decomposition and targeted search. The model leverages uncertainty estimates to decide when to stop gathering evidence. We evaluate effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness under corrupted evidence and domain shifts. Our contributions include a new training protocol, comprehensive ablations, and strong results under constrained budgets.

document: Object detection aims to localize and classify objects in images for a broad spectrum of applications. Modern detectors fall into anchor based and anchor free paradigms, each with distinct design biases (Tian et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2015). Deformable convolutions adapt sampling locations to object geometry and have become a powerful building block (Dai et al., 2017). document: Federated learning enables training models across many clients without centralizing raw data (McMahan et al., 2017). We identify common pitfalls and propose a standardized, reproducible setup with clear separation between training, validation, and testing. spans: dominant
document: Open-domain question answering (ODQA) aims to produce answers to natural language questions using large collections. Early systems retrieved passages and extracted answers with symbolic or lexical methods. Neural approaches introduced dense representations for documents and questions. Retriever-reader pipelines separate evidence acquisition from answer synthesis. Dense retrievers map queries and passages to a shared space to enable efficient search. Readers then condition on a small set of retrieved texts to extract or generate answers. Some studies incorporate knowledge distillation between retrievers and readers. Other lines explore multi-hop reasoning over linked documents. Memory-augmented architectures cache intermediate facts for iterative refinement. Generative models have also been adapted to perform implicit retrieval via parametric memory. Despite these variations, system design often converges on a small number of templates. Within this landscape, the two-stage retriever-reader architecture is dominant in open-domain question answering systems. This configuration simplifies engineering by modularizing retrieval and comprehension. It also aligns with available infrastructure for indexing and serving large corpora. However, modularity can exacerbate exposure bias between retriever and reader. Pipeline errors in retrieval are difficult to recover from during reading. Conversely, end-to-end differentiable retrieval promises joint optimization. Yet joint methods raise new challenges in scalability and stability. Evaluation practices typically assess exact match and token-level F1 on curated benchmarks. We also report calibration and abstention analyses. Efficiency metrics such as latency and index size affect deployability. Domain shift remains a critical obstacle for real-world deployment. In this study we revisit retriever-reader interaction with lightweight interfaces. We introduce adapters that allow the reader to query the retriever during inference. Our experiments assess whether these interfaces mitigate exposure bias without sacrificing speed. The results motivate a reexamination of how evidence is shared across retrieval and reading.

document: Automated segmentation is central to quantitative analysis in medical imaging. Clinical workflows rely on delineations of organs, tumors, and vessels for planning and prognosis. Traditional methods used handcrafted features and deformable models. Deep learning has transformed segmentation with convolutional encoders and decoders. Skip connections help retain spatial detail while expanding receptive fields. Attention modules refine features by focusing on relevant regions. We discuss multi-scale fusion to capture context at different resolutions. Transformer backbones extend receptive fields with self-attention on tokenized patches. Hybrid CNN–Transformer models aim to balance locality and global reasoning. We consider self-supervised pretraining to leverage large unlabeled archives and mitigate annotation costs. We also examine semi-supervised learning that exploits unlabeled cases with consistency objectives. Despite these advances, clinical deployment requires robustness and interpretability. In practice, U shaped encoder–decoder architectures are canonical for biomedical image segmentation. Their contracting and expanding paths support precise localization after global encoding. Variants introduce residual blocks to ease optimization in deep networks. Others incorporate pyramid pooling to aggregate context. Lightweight models target embedded ultrasound and intraoperative scenarios. Loss functions such as Dice and focal terms handle class imbalance and small structures. Postprocessing with connected components can reduce spurious detections. Our evaluation reports Dice, Hausdorff distance, and volume error across folds. We include cross-site validation to assess generalization to different scanners and protocols. We investigate a modular framework that decouples representation learning from topology priors. Our approach injects differentiable topology constraints via persistent homology losses. We also design plug-in adapters for cross-modality transfer. Experiments span MRI, CT, and ultrasound datasets with varied pathologies. The study aims to clarify when topology priors improve clinical reliability.

document: Static program analysis seeks to infer properties of source code without execution. Applications include vulnerability detection, type inference, and code optimization. Neural models have been adapted to represent programs as graphs. Control flow graphs and abstract syntax trees provide complementary views of code. Graph neural networks propagate messages along edges to encode structural context. Path-based encoders aggregate evidence across long-range dependencies. Pretraining on large code corpora supplies transferable representations across tasks. Contrastive objectives align code snippets that share semantics. Our benchmarks include tasks spanning multiple languages and paradigms. Model portability across toolchains remains a practical concern. For many tasks, message-passing encoders over ASTs and CFGs are prevailing design choices in the literature. These encoders support flow-sensitive reasoning when combined with path context. Neural analyzers also incorporate symbolic information such as types and call graphs. Hybrid neuro-symbolic systems aim to capture both precise rules and statistical cues. Representations can be augmented with API usage graphs and pointer relations. Efficient batching strategies are required for large programs with millions of edges. Evaluation protocols compare classification accuracy and AUROC where appropriate. Error analysis highlights missed long-range dependencies and aliasing effects. We focus on a modular architecture that isolates message passing from readout heads. Our design enables swapping in different graph encoders without retraining the task heads. We introduce a path scheduler that allocates computation based on control-flow complexity. The scheduler reduces unnecessary propagation in locally linear regions. Experiments examine cross-language transfer and ablations on structural features. Results indicate benefits of the scheduler for deep propagation budgets.

document: Neural text-to-speech systems aim to synthesize natural audio from text. Early end-to-end approaches modeled mel-spectrograms followed by neural vocoders. Autoregressive models achieved high fidelity but suffered from slow sampling. Normalizing flows enabled faster inference with tractable likelihoods. Recently, diffusion models have been adapted to speech generation. They denoise latent variables over many steps to yield high-quality waveforms. Conditioning mechanisms incorporate phonemes, durations, and speaker embeddings. Alignment strategies learn correspondences between text tokens and acoustic frames. Prosody modeling remains challenging due to variability in emphasis and rhythm. Cross-speaker transfer requires disentangling content and timbre. Training stability depends on noise schedules and variance parameterization. Curriculum strategies adjust step counts to balance quality and speed. In modern pipelines, flow-matching diffusion decoders are ubiquitous for high-fidelity TTS. These decoders can be paired with lightweight text encoders for efficient conditioning. Non-autoregressive designs reduce latency by synthesizing frames in parallel. Discrete tokenization of audio with vector quantization provides compact conditioning. Joint training of the encoder and decoder can improve alignment quality. Distillation reduces the number of sampling steps at inference time. Evaluation considers MOS, word error rate under ASR, and pitch statistics. Robustness tests probe noise, punctuation, and out-of-domain text. We present a modular TTS stack built around a controllable diffusion decoder. The model exposes interfaces for style tokens, duration controls, and lexical stress. We propose a pacing loss that modulates denoising according to local duration. This loss stabilizes alignment without sacrificing prosodic variation. Experiments compare distillation schedules and conditioning granularities. The findings highlight trade-offs between speed, controllability, and robustness.

document: Instruction tuning aligns pretrained language models to follow natural language directives. The approach reframes tasks as instruction input and expected output pairs. Mixtures of tasks expose models to varied formats and domains. Supervision may be curated from existing datasets or synthesized automatically. Preference learning further refines outputs to be helpful and safe. Data scaling trades off breadth of skills against noise and redundancy. Mixture design influences generalization to previously unseen instructions. Tokenization and template choices affect instruction understanding. Evaluation spans held-out tasks, adversarial prompts, and human judgments. Safety analyses probe failure modes and content policy adherence. Open-source checkpoints enable reproducible baselines across scales. Smaller models benefit from distillation from stronger teachers. In many systems, instruction tuning on FLAN-style and Super-Natural Instructions mixtures is standard practice. These mixtures provide diverse exemplars of formatting and reasoning. Chain-of-thought variants inject intermediate rationales into the targets. Self-instruct procedures bootstrap additional tasks from seed prompts. Preference optimization translates pairwise or listwise comparisons into learning signals. Token-level losses can be augmented with reward models during fine-tuning. Evaluation reports accuracy on composite suites and calibration under distribution shift. We also measure refusal quality and harmlessness in safety-focused subsets. Our method isolates mixture composition effects from model size confounds. We vary instruction categories while holding sampling temperature fixed. The study decomposes gains attributable to verbalization, coverage, and curriculum. We analyze how template entropy correlates with zero-shot extrapolation. Experiments include ablations on rationale density and negative sampling. The results inform robust recipe design for future instruction-tuning efforts.

spans: Recent studies predominantly adopt a two stage retriever reader pipeline for biomedical question answering in clinical settings.
document: Biomedical question answering aims to return clinically relevant facts given natural language queries. Accurate systems can support evidence based practice and accelerate literature review workflows in hospitals. Open domain settings are commonly studied alongside closed domain variants in the biomedical literature (Lee et al., 2019; Wiese et al., 2017). Key corpora include PubMed abstracts and PubMed Central articles that provide large scale scientific text (Fiorini et al., 2017). Domain specific terminology, abbreviations, and negation pose persistent challenges for retrieval and reasoning (Zhang et al., 2020). Pretrained language models specialized for biomedical text have improved representation quality and downstream accuracy (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Retrieval augmented generation has emerged as a competitive strategy for fact seeking tasks in specialized domains (Lewis et al., 2020; Guu et al., 2020). Dense passage retrievers trained with in batch negatives often outperform sparse term matching for literature search (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). Generative readers based on encoder decoder architectures can synthesize answers while conditioning on retrieved evidence (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Roberts et al., 2020). Biomedical QA evaluation frequently relies on factoid and list style questions from the BioASQ challenge (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015; Nentidis et al., 2020). Despite progress, systems can hallucinate unsupported statements that undermine clinical trustworthiness (Maynez et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2023). Prior surveys outline design choices across retrieval, reranking, and answer generation components in specialized QA (Möller et al., 2022). Recent studies predominantly adopt a two stage retriever reader pipeline for biomedical question answering in clinical settings. This design couples retrieval and extraction tightly, which can raise latency and memory demands for deployment. End to end retrieval generation methods have been explored using differentiable memory and k nearest neighbor fusion (Kassner et al., 2020; Shuster et al., 2021). Complementary lines integrate ontological signals such as UMLS concepts to improve grounding and disambiguation (Sung et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2022). Contrastive pretraining with citation links has also been used to shape representations of scientific discourse (Cohan et al., 2020; Wadden et al., 2020). Datasets beyond BioASQ, including PubMedQA and MedQA, broaden the question formats and difficulty levels (Jin et al., 2019; Jin et al., 2021). Automatic metrics span exact match, F1, and n gram overlap but often fail to capture factual consistency (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Fabbri et al., 2021). Human assessment protocols have been proposed to judge evidence attribution and medical harm potential (Koto et al., 2022). In this work we prioritize verifiable answering with explicit evidence citations as a first class objective. Our approach combines a dense retriever with cross encoder reranking to improve passage selection quality (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). We incorporate terminology expansion using synonym graphs induced from UMLS relations to mitigate vocabulary mismatch (Bodenreider, 2004). We evaluate on BioASQ factoid tasks and PubMedQA yes no questions under standard splits (Tsatsaronis et al., 2015; Jin et al., 2019). Our contributions include a retrieval calibration procedure that balances recall with reader robustness. We also release ablations and error analyses that highlight remaining gaps in factual grounding for biomedical QA.

spans: Most previous systems fine tune bert like encoders with span based tagging for aspect extraction in reviews.
document: Aspect based sentiment analysis seeks to determine sentiments toward specific entities or attributes within text. This granularity is essential for customer feedback analytics where multiple opinions may appear in a single review. Classical pipelines decompose the task into aspect extraction and sentiment classification with sequence labeling and classification heads (Pontiki et al., 2014; Ma et al., 2018). Pretrained transformers have achieved strong results when adapted to domain annotated corpora for this task (Devlin et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Distant supervision has been explored to expand training data through review ratings and heuristic segment selection (He et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2021). Joint modeling approaches capture interactions between target mentions and sentiment indicators to reduce error propagation (Li et al., 2019; Chen and Qian, 2020). Graph based encoders leverage syntactic dependencies to improve aspect identification and polarity detection (Zhang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020). Cross domain transfer remains challenging due to vocabulary drift and domain specific expressions common in user generated content (Barnes et al., 2018). Multi task learning has been applied to share representations among related subtasks and promote generalization (He et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2021). Prompt based methods have recently reframed extraction and classification as constrained generation with template verbalizers (Li et al., 2022; Mao et al., 2022). Continual learning settings arise when models must incorporate new product aspects over time without catastrophic forgetting (De Lange et al., 2021). Evaluation benchmarks typically include the SemEval series and domain specific review datasets with fine grained annotations (Pontiki et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2020). Resource constraints motivate lightweight adaptation techniques that avoid full parameter fine tuning in production environments (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2021). Most previous systems fine tune bert like encoders with span based tagging for aspect extraction in reviews. However, such choices can entangle aspect boundaries with noisy tokenization artifacts in informal text. Recent work investigates span free formulations that directly predict start end indices using pointer networks (Gao et al., 2021). Others cast the problem as question answering by asking model generated queries about candidate aspects (McAuley et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022). A complementary direction designs contrastive objectives to separate aspect specific cues from background context (Chen et al., 2022). Domain adaptation has been pursued via adversarial training to align latent spaces across product categories (Ganin et al., 2016; Rietzler et al., 2020). Knowledge enhanced models exploit sentiment lexicons and commonsense resources to resolve implicit targets and polarities (Ma et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2020). We address robustness to negation, intensifiers, and multi target interactions under limited supervision. Our method introduces adapter modules that condition on syntactic spans without updating backbone parameters (Pfeiffer et al., 2021). We incorporate a calibration step that aligns confidence with expected calibration error across domains (Guo et al., 2017). Experiments cover SemEval 2014 restaurant and laptop datasets with standard splits and metrics (Pontiki et al., 2014). The approach yields gains in macro F1 and aspect level accuracy while reducing variance across seeds. We release code and configuration files to facilitate reproducible research and future comparison.

spans: Prior work typically employs graph convolution with Bayesian personalized ranking loss on implicit feedback data at scale.
document: Learning to recommend items from implicit feedback often exploits user item interaction graphs. Such graphs encode collaborative signals beyond simple co occurrence counts important for ranking quality. Matrix factorization provided early baselines under pairwise ranking objectives in large scale settings (Rendle et al., 2009). Neural collaborative filtering introduced nonlinear interaction functions to capture higher order effects (He et al., 2017). Graph neural networks generalize neighborhood aggregation to propagate preference signals along edges (Kipf and Welling, 2017; Hamilton et al., 2017). Several methods design lightweight propagation schemes to handle high degree nodes and long range dependencies (Wu et al., 2019; Ying et al., 2018). Negative sampling strategies strongly influence the learned ranking margin on sparse interactions (Bayer et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020). Self supervised pretraining with auxiliary views has improved cold start performance for new users and items (Xia et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2020). Session based recommendation has benefited from temporal graph encoders and attention mechanisms for short term intent (Wang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2017). Evaluation typically reports recall and normalized discounted cumulative gain at fixed cutoffs on public logs (Harper and Konstan, 2015; Sun et al., 2019). Industrial deployments emphasize memory efficiency and fast incremental updates to accommodate evolving catalogs (Chiang et al., 2019). Reranking with diversified objectives can balance relevance with coverage under business constraints (Ashkan et al., 2015). Prior work typically employs graph convolution with Bayesian personalized ranking loss on implicit feedback data at scale. However, this practice can over smooth representations and dampen personalization in dense neighborhoods. Recent variants adopt residual designs and feature dropout to mitigate over smoothing without sacrificing depth (Chen et al., 2020; Rong et al., 2020). Contrastive objectives between local and global views further enhance representation separation for popular items (Qu et al., 2021). Some studies incorporate knowledge graph relations to capture semantic connections among entities and attributes (Wang et al., 2019). Others integrate sequential signals by combining graph encoders with transformers over interaction timelines (Kang and McAuley, 2018; Sun et al., 2019). Online learning protocols address distribution shift by updating embeddings with mini batch streaming data (Zhang et al., 2020). Exploration strategies based on bandits can reduce popularity bias while collecting informative negatives (Li et al., 2010; Zheng et al., 2020). We focus on maintaining ranking quality under strict memory budgets in dynamic catalogs. Our method constructs compact item clusters and limits propagation to representative centroids to reduce redundancy. We propose an adaptive sampling rule that emphasizes informative inter cluster edges while preserving user diversity. Experiments on MovieLens and Amazon product data follow standard preprocessing and evaluation pipelines (Harper and Konstan, 2015; Ni et al., 2019). Results demonstrate improved recall at fixed memory limits compared with competitive compressed baselines. Ablation studies isolate the impact of centroid selection and sampling aggressiveness on generalization.

document: Neural machine translation quality degrades when test data diverge from the training domain. Domain adaptation seeks to restore accuracy through targeted data and optimization strategies. Fine tuning on small in domain corpora remains a strong baseline for adapting pretrained models (Luong and Manning, 2015). Data selection approaches identify relevant subsets from large generic corpora using cross entropy or contrastive criteria (Moore and Lewis, 2010; Wang et al., 2017). Synthetic data generation augments scarce domains through round trip translation and noise injection (Sennrich et al., 2016; Edunov et al., 2018). Continued pretraining on in domain monolingual text has shown gains for both encoder and decoder components (Gururangan et al., 2020; Aharoni and Goldberg, 2020). Multidomain models with domain tags enable parameter sharing while conditioning on domain specific signals (Kobus et al., 2017; Britz et al., 2017). Adapters provide lightweight mechanisms for specializing a common backbone to multiple domains (Bapna and Firat, 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Vocabulary mismatch and terminology drift are persistent barriers in technical and colloquial domains alike (Koehn and Knowles, 2017). Evaluation often uses WMT news test sets and domain specific benchmarks such as IT and medical corpora (Bojar et al., 2017; Koehn, 2005). Risk sensitive training has been explored to emphasize domain critical errors over generic n gram overlap (Shen et al., 2016). Lexicon constrained decoding can enforce terminology consistency in specialized translation scenarios (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018). Recent studies rely on back translation and iterative self training to adapt models across domains with success. Nevertheless, iterative schemes can amplify noise when pseudo labels contain systematic biases from the seed model. Several works filter synthetic pairs using confidence and language model scores to mitigate noise propagation (Wang et al., 2018; Xu and Koehn, 2021). Other methods exploit bilingual lexicons and term banks to anchor translations of domain specific terminology (Dinu et al., 2019). Continual learning approaches prevent catastrophic forgetting when models are sequentially adapted to new domains (Thompson et al., 2019; Ke et al., 2021). A complementary line learns domain discriminators to guide representation separation during multitask training (Britz et al., 2017; Chu and Wang, 2018). We report BLEU, chrF, and terminology accuracy to quantify both fluency and fidelity (Papineni et al., 2002; Popovic, 2015). Comparative results show consistent gains over strong baselines across language pairs and domains. Analyses reveal improved handling of rare terms and fewer unconstrained deviations from provided glossaries. We release code, models, and configuration files to facilitate replicability and deployment.

spans: Most evaluations center on HumanEval and MBPP with unit test based metrics and prompt conditioning in practice.
document: Program synthesis from natural language has advanced rapidly with large language models trained on code corpora. Despite progress, rigorous evaluation remains challenging due to the diversity of tasks and specifications. Early work framed code generation as conditional language modeling with sequence to sequence architectures (Ling et al., 2016; Yin and Neubig, 2017). Pretraining on GitHub scale datasets has yielded strong zero shot and few shot capabilities for coding tasks (Chen et al., 2021; Nijkamp et al., 2022). Retrieval augmented modeling can further ground generation in relevant library usage and idiomatic patterns (Parvez et al., 2021). Inference time prompting techniques influence solution quality through demonstrations and test driven feedback (Brown et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2023). Safety concerns arise from potential insertion of vulnerable patterns or license restricted snippets into generated code (Pearce et al., 2021). Dataset curation and de duplication strategies impact both pretraining quality and downstream benchmark validity (Allamanis, 2019; Li et al., 2022). Static analysis and type checking provide complementary signals to judge semantic correctness beyond surface forms (Pradel and Sen, 2018). Automatic evaluations often rely on executing generated code against hidden unit tests to determine pass rates (Austin et al., 2021). Newer protocols analyze solution diversity and partial credit by measuring individual test coverage and mutation scores (Lachaux et al., 2021). Benchmarks vary in task granularity, language coverage, and docstring cleanliness, leading to comparability concerns (Liu et al., 2023). Prompt sensitivity and stochastic decoding complicate claims of superiority unless evaluation seeds and prompts are controlled (Dehghani et al., 2021). Post processing with canonicalization and import normalization can reduce spurious runtime errors in evaluation harnesses (Zhang et al., 2023). Most evaluations center on HumanEval and MBPP with unit test based metrics and prompt conditioning in practice. However, these suites emphasize short functions and may not capture integration with larger project contexts. Additional benchmarks target data science notebooks and competitive programming tasks with richer constraints (Austin et al., 2021; Li et al., 2023). Repair oriented settings test the ability to fix buggy functions under partial specifications and tests (Dinella et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2021). Multilingual code generation introduces further challenges with libraries, tooling, and idioms across ecosystems (Puri et al., 2021). Evaluations must also consider resource usage when compiling and executing potentially expensive programs at scale (Chen et al., 2021). We investigate robustness to prompt paraphrases and variable renamings under controlled decoding regimes. Our method aggregates multiple diverse candidates and selects solutions using lightweight test generalization proxies. We complement pass rates with coverage based diagnostics and specification adherence measures derived from docstrings. Experiments include HumanEval, MBPP, and a curated subset of competitive programming problems with public tests (Chen et al., 2021; Austin et al., 2021). Results show consistent gains under deterministic and stochastic decoding while reducing variance across seeds. We release code and evaluation scripts to enable transparent and repeatable comparisons.

spans: Recent studies uniformly report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines trained on MS MARCO have become the de facto standard for English ODQA evaluation across news and Wikipedia corpora.
document: Open-domain question answering has evolved rapidly with advances in dense retrieval and generative readers (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Early systems relied on sparse lexical indices combined with extractive readers for answer span prediction (Chen et al., 2017). The advent of bi-encoder retrievers improved recall by learning semantic representations over large corpora (Lee et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020). Generative architectures further exploited retrieved evidence to synthesize answers with broader abstraction (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Training data selection has been shown to crucially affect retrieval robustness across domains (Zhu et al., 2021; Oguz et al., 2022). Hard negative mining and in-batch negatives consistently strengthen retriever discrimination (Xiong et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021). Reader models benefit from improved calibration and reranking strategies during inference (Nogueira et al., 2019; Kadavath et al., 2022). Multi-hop settings require explicit reasoning over linked passages to compose supporting facts (Yang et al., 2018; Qi et al., 2020). Cross-lingual ODQA introduces additional challenges in document alignment and retrieval transfer (Asai et al., 2021). Efficient retrieval with approximate nearest neighbor indices has enabled real-time question answering at web scale (Johnson et al., 2019; Guo et al., 2020). In parallel, unsupervised retrievers attempt to sidestep costly labeling through contrastive pretraining on raw corpora (Gao and Callan, 2021). Recent studies uniformly report that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines trained on MS MARCO have become the de facto standard for English ODQA evaluation across news and Wikipedia corpora. Despite strong benchmarks, evidence aggregation remains fragile when passages contain conflicting statements or temporal drift (Lazaridou et al., 2021; Liska et al., 2022). Attribution-aware generation aims to keep answers grounded in cited passages to mitigate hallucinations (Rashkin et al., 2021; Menick et al., 2022). Query rewriting has been explored to better align user intents with retriever-friendly formulations (Vakulenko et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021). Passage rerankers using cross-encoders often deliver state-of-the-art top-k precision at higher computational cost (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Humeau et al., 2020). Scaling retrievers with larger encoders and more negatives continues to yield steady gains (Luan et al., 2021; Santhanam et al., 2022). Knowledge distillation from powerful readers into retrievers tightens the retriever-reader interface (Izacard and Grave, 2021; Oguz et al., 2022). Domain adaptation approaches target specialized corpora such as biomedicine and law without extensive relabeling (Lee et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Evaluation metrics increasingly consider calibration and faithfulness alongside exact match accuracy (Stiennon et al., 2020; Kamoi et al., 2023). Adversarial retrieval stresses systems with paraphrastic distractors to test robustness under lexical variation (Jia and Liang, 2017; Bartolo et al., 2020). Joint training of retriever and reader seeks end-to-end improvements through shared gradients and objectives (Guu et al., 2020). Memory-augmented models provide long-term caches that personalize knowledge while preserving generalization (Borgeaud et al., 2022). Recent generative search frameworks reinterpret retrieval as latent variable modeling within sequence decoders (Bevilacqua et al., 2022). Nevertheless, retrieval latency and energy footprints pose practical deployment bottlenecks in production systems (Shi et al., 2023). Compression via product quantization and knowledge distillation reduces memory without drastic accuracy loss (Jégou et al., 2011; Lin et al., 2020). Continual learning is essential to keep pace with evolving world knowledge and dynamic corpora (Lazaridou et al., 2021). Safety constraints further require filtering ungrounded or sensitive content in both retrieval and generation (Solaiman and Dennison, 2021). Our work revisits retriever-reader coupling with a focus on evidence consolidation and post-hoc attribution. We present a framework that integrates multi-source retrieval, calibrated generation, and dataset-aware evaluation to advance reliable ODQA.
reason: The span generalizes about prior work and trends, references a specific method and dataset (two-stage retriever-reader, MS MARCO), and provides no citations or evidence.

spans: Prior work widely treats CNN/DailyMail as the universal benchmark for abstractive summarization and reports gains using pointer-generator architectures with coverage penalties across nearly all topic domains today.
document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense documents into fluent digests while preserving salient facts (Nallapati et al., 2016; See et al., 2017). Neural sequence-to-sequence models have driven significant performance improvements on news benchmarks (Rush et al., 2015; Paulus et al., 2018). Pretrained encoder-decoder architectures form the current backbone for many systems (Lewis et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020). However, hallucinations and unsupported statements remain persistent challenges for reliability (Maynez et al., 2020; Pagnoni et al., 2021). Content planning strategies attempt to select and order facts before generation to mitigate drift (Hsu et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2021). Controllability via prompts and planning tags has also been explored for style and content fidelity (He et al., 2020; Dou et al., 2021). Faithfulness metrics leverage information extraction and entailment models to diagnose factual errors (Kryscinski et al., 2020; Goyal and Durrett, 2021). Post-editing approaches use constrained decoding and copy mechanisms to enforce source alignment (Miao and Blunsom, 2016; Cao et al., 2018). Multi-document summarization presents additional aggregation challenges and cross-source consistency issues (Liu and Lapata, 2019; Fabbri et al., 2019). Domain-specific summarization requires adapting models to biomedical, legal, and scientific corpora (DeYoung et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). Long-form summarization introduces memory constraints and theme tracking difficulties (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). Human evaluation remains crucial, yet it is expensive and subject to annotator variation (Fabbri et al., 2021). Hybrid metrics seek better correlation with human judgments by combining precision and recall of facts (Durmus et al., 2020; Laban et al., 2022). Pretraining on discourse-rich objectives has recently boosted factual consistency in generation (Goyal and Durrett, 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Data augmentation through paraphrasing and back-translation can improve robustness to lexical variation (Sennrich et al., 2016; Fadaee et al., 2017). Prior work widely treats CNN/DailyMail as the universal benchmark for abstractive summarization and reports gains using pointer-generator architectures with coverage penalties across nearly all topic domains today. Yet, news-style data may not reflect the complexities of user-authored or technical content (Thompson and Reitter, 2020). Recent shared tasks emphasize factual grounding and source attribution to encourage safer models (Gabriel et al., 2020; Kryscinski et al., 2020). Efficient finetuning techniques reduce computational costs for domain adaptation (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Contrastive objectives that distinguish entailed from contradicted sentences show promise for error reduction (Nan et al., 2021). Alignment learning with structured extractors encourages summaries to preserve entity relations (Zhou et al., 2021). Calibration methods adjust confidence scores to better signal potential hallucinations at inference time (Kadavath et al., 2022). Source-aware decoding penalizes unsupported tokens based on attention agreement heuristics (Goodrich et al., 2019). Prompting large language models with exemplars offers strong zero-shot baselines with minimal task engineering (Brown et al., 2020). Nevertheless, they can overgeneralize and omit crucial details without targeted constraints (Honovich et al., 2022). Benchmark diversity and transparent reporting are essential to assess generalization beyond news domains (Gehrmann et al., 2021). We focus on factuality for structured, technical documents that contain dense terminology and cross-references. Our dataset pairs procedural articles with expert-written key statements to guide evaluation. We propose an extraction-guided decoder that conditions on verified propositions during generation. Experiments demonstrate improved factual precision and competitive summaries under human and automatic assessments.
reason: The span asserts a broad characterization of prior work, mentions a specific dataset and method trend (CNN/DailyMail, pointer-generator with coverage), and offers no citations, thus unsupported.

spans: Most prior work frames relation extraction as a pipeline of BERT-based entity taggers followed by a biaffine relation classifier, evaluated on TACRED and DocRED under sentence-level settings.
document: Information extraction seeks to structure unstructured text into entities and relations for downstream applications (Grishman, 2015). Neural approaches now dominate relation extraction due to effective pretrained representations (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). Supervised datasets provide clear benchmarks but involve costly annotation processes (Zhang et al., 2017; Alt et al., 2020). Distant supervision leverages knowledge bases to scale training with noisy labels (Mintz et al., 2009; Riedel et al., 2010). Multi-instance learning mitigates noise by aggregating sentence bags for relation decisions (Surdeanu et al., 2012; Lin et al., 2016). Dependency-based contextualization improves pattern sensitivity for long-distance relations (Zhang et al., 2018; Fu et al., 2019). Document-level relation extraction models handle cross-sentence reasoning with graph structures (Yao et al., 2019; Nan et al., 2020). Prompt-based tuning has recently been applied to reduce task-specific parameters (Gao et al., 2021; Schick and Schütze, 2021). Span-based entity models jointly consider boundaries and types in a unified framework (Luan et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020). Calibration and uncertainty estimation remain underexplored for reliable extraction (Zhou et al., 2022). Evaluation protocols vary widely across datasets, complicating holistic comparison (Takanobu et al., 2020). Robustness studies indicate degradation under entity perturbations and domain shift (Alt et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Pretraining on scientific text improves extraction in specialized domains such as biomedicine (Beltagy et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020). Most prior work frames relation extraction as a pipeline of BERT-based entity taggers followed by a biaffine relation classifier, evaluated on TACRED and DocRED under sentence-level settings. Joint modeling of entities and relations has been explored to reduce error propagation (Miwa and Bansal, 2016; Wadden et al., 2019). Graph neural networks encode entity mentions and dependency edges for global reasoning (Zhang et al., 2018; Zeng et al., 2020). Adversarial training seeks invariance to lexical shortcuts that obscure causal features (Zhou et al., 2019). Contrastive objectives align entity pair representations with relation semantics across instances (Chen et al., 2020). Denoising strategies reweight or filter noisy distant labels using curriculum or expectation maximization (Zhou et al., 2016; Qin et al., 2018). Knowledge base constraints can regularize predictions and enforce type compatibility (Guo et al., 2020). Few-shot relation extraction transfers knowledge via meta-learning and prototype induction (Han et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019). Inference-time retrieval of definitions augments models with external knowledge for rare relations (Levine et al., 2020). Domain adaptation through feature alignment improves cross-corpus generalization (Peng et al., 2017). Tokenization and sentence segmentation errors introduce cascading failures in pipelines (Daza and Frank, 2020). Evaluation must account for partial overlap and directionality ambiguities in extracted triples (Taillé et al., 2020). Recent shared tasks emphasize realistic noise and cross-document reasoning in open settings (Ji et al., 2020). Our focus is joint extraction with uncertainty estimates to better prioritize human review. We curate a challenging corpus with long documents and imbalanced relation types. The proposed model integrates span detection, relation reasoning, and calibration under a unified loss. We show improvements in precision, robustness, and selective prediction across multiple datasets.

spans: Recent studies commonly evaluate code generation with pass@k on HumanEval and MBPP while using retrieval-augmented decoders to import library snippets during synthesis and testing in controlled sandboxes.
document: Program synthesis from natural language has gained momentum with large language models trained on code corpora (Chen et al., 2021; Nijkamp et al., 2023). The task frames code generation as conditioned sequence modeling over programming languages (Austin et al., 2021). Training data scale and deduplication significantly influence generalization and memorization (Allamanis, 2019; Li et al., 2022). Static analysis and execution feedback offer signals for grounded generation and repair (Le et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2023). Prompt design affects function signatures, dependency imports, and coding style (Liu et al., 2023). Chain-of-thought prompting has been adapted to reason about algorithms and edge cases (Wei et al., 2022). Unit-test driven decoding encourages test-aware program synthesis with iterative refinement (Austin et al., 2021; Le et al., 2022). Execution-based reranking improves correctness by filtering compilable and passing candidates (Zhong et al., 2023). Safety concerns include insecure patterns and data exfiltration hazards in generated code (Pearce et al., 2022). Dataset contamination can inflate reported results unless training overlaps are thoroughly audited (Magar and Schwartz, 2022). Robust evaluation requires multiple languages and diverse libraries to avoid shortcut exploitation (Liu et al., 2023). Natural language ambiguity remains a core source of failure in algorithmic synthesis (Kulal et al., 2019). Retrieval augmentation has been proposed to surface relevant API usage and idioms (Shi et al., 2023). Self-consistency and majority voting often boost pass rates without architectural changes (Wang et al., 2022). Post-hoc repair models rewrite failed candidates conditioned on error traces (Yasunaga and Liang, 2020). Human-in-the-loop workflows can prioritize risky code segments for review (Vaithilingam et al., 2022). Instruction tuning on curated datasets improves adherence to developer intent (Ouyang et al., 2022). Recent studies commonly evaluate code generation with pass@k on HumanEval and MBPP while using retrieval-augmented decoders to import library snippets during synthesis and testing in controlled sandboxes. Multi-turn code assistants introduce memory and tool-use considerations for interactive sessions (Shinn et al., 2023). Program repair benchmarks measure patch correctness beyond superficial token overlap (Monperrus, 2018). Static security analyzers can be integrated as discriminators to steer generation (Pearce et al., 2022). Toolformer-style approaches enable models to call external tools when needed (Schick et al., 2023). Few-shot demonstrations show strong gains on niche libraries with minimal additional data (Brown et al., 2020). However, few studies probe long-horizon tasks requiring multi-module coordination and stateful execution (Yin et al., 2023). We examine algorithmically demanding problems with explicit constraints and hidden tests. Our benchmark includes graded difficulty, library variety, and security-sensitive scenarios. The proposed system uses retrieval, planning, and execution feedback in a unified loop. We design evaluation that separates memorization from reasoning by excluding near-duplicates. Results indicate improved reliability and reduced vulnerability introduction compared to strong baselines. We release code and evaluation harnesses to facilitate reproducible research.

spans: It is widely accepted that CLIP-style contrastive pretraining on LAION-400M is the default initialization for downstream vision-language tasks such as VQAv2 and COCO Captioning in most pipelines.
document: Vision-language pretraining aims to learn aligned representations from paired images and text (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Contrastive objectives match modalities by bringing positive pairs closer in embedding space (Radford et al., 2021). Masked modeling reconstructs corrupted tokens or patches to encourage joint reasoning (Bao et al., 2022; He et al., 2022). Cross-modal attention layers fuse visual and textual information for downstream transfer (Li et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Large-scale web data has proved effective for broad coverage of objects and concepts (Jia et al., 2021). However, noisy captions and spurious correlations can hurt robustness and fairness (Wang et al., 2021; Goyal et al., 2022). Debiasing strategies include balanced sampling, counterfactual augmentation, and adversarial objectives (Zhao et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). Prompting and adapters enable parameter-efficient transfer to diverse tasks (Gao et al., 2021; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented training incorporates hard negatives to sharpen contrastive learning (Xiong et al., 2020). Multimodal knowledge distillation compresses large teachers into lightweight students for deployment (Gou et al., 2021). Fine-grained grounding is evaluated by phrase localization and referring expression comprehension (Yu et al., 2018; Plummer et al., 2015). Zero-shot recognition highlights the open-vocabulary capabilities of aligned models (Radford et al., 2021). VQA remains a central benchmark for reasoning over images and questions (Goyal et al., 2017; Antol et al., 2015). Image captioning assesses generative fluency and semantic coverage with automatic metrics and human ratings (Chen et al., 2015; Vedantam et al., 2015). Advancements in transformer backbones and patch-level tokenization improved scalability for high-resolution inputs (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). Structured pretraining tasks leverage scene graphs and object tags to provide inductive bias (Li et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2022). Temporal extensions handle video question answering and captioning with hierarchical encoders (Lei et al., 2020; Zellers et al., 2021). Safety concerns involve harmful content recognition and privacy preservation for images and text (Birhane and Prabhu, 2021). Evaluation protocols increasingly consider calibration and compositional generalization for robust deployment (Yuksekgonul et al., 2022). It is widely accepted that CLIP-style contrastive pretraining on LAION-400M is the default initialization for downstream vision-language tasks such as VQAv2 and COCO Captioning in most pipelines. Despite strong zero-shot results, compositional failures persist when novel attribute combinations appear (Misra et al., 2017). Language biases can lead models to ignore images when dataset priors suffice (Agrawal et al., 2018). Counterfactual data augmentation aims to disentangle correlations between words and visual cues (Niu et al., 2021). Training on multilingual captions extends coverage to non-English queries and labels (Ni et al., 2021). Cross-dataset evaluation reveals instability due to annotation and style differences (Shankar et al., 2020). Open-world detection integrates class expansion with continual learning objectives (Gupta et al., 2020). We investigate alignment under fine-grained attribute composition with limited supervision. Our method uses structured prompting and region-level contrast with debiased sampling. We evaluate on VQA, captioning, and retrieval with compositional splits and diagnostic sets. Results show improved generalization without sacrificing zero-shot recognition quality.
