spans: standard
document: Open-domain question answering has evolved rapidly over the past decade. Early systems coupled symbolic retrieval with hand engineered answer scoring. The advent of neural encoders enabled dense retrieval and neural readers. Hybrid pipelines combine these components in various ways. Our focus is on methods that balance efficiency and accuracy in large corpora. Prior work established the value of weak supervision for retriever training (Lee et al., 2019). Others leveraged distant supervision from hyperlinks and tables to expand coverage (Chen et al., 2017; Oguz et al., 2020). Knowledge augmented readers have also been explored to inject structured signals (Dhingra et al., 2020). End to end differentiable retrieval has shown promise for niche domains (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Despite these advances, scalability remains a core challenge at web scale. Index refresh and continual learning introduce distribution shift issues (Agarwal et al., 2022). Evaluation practices vary widely across datasets and metrics (Min et al., 2021). Our study revisits these questions through a unified experimental protocol. We analyze the role of document granularity and negative sampling. We also probe robustness to adversarial paraphrases and entity swaps. We contextualize results by controlling for annotation artifacts in the benchmarks (Sulem et al., 2021). In current systems, the two stage retriever reader architecture is now standard in open domain QA. Yet the effectiveness of this configuration depends strongly on training regimes. For retrievers, contrastive objectives and in batch negatives are common choices (Xiong et al., 2021). For readers, span extraction and generative modeling exhibit complementary strengths (Roberts et al., 2020). Multi hop reasoning introduces additional challenges around evidence aggregation (Yang et al., 2018). Recent benchmarks emphasize attribution and faithful rationales (DeYoung et al., 2020). However, most corpora still concentrate on Wikipedia like sources (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020). We therefore include news, forums, and scientific text in our study. We assess latency using both CPU and GPU inference. We measure memory footprint under varying index sizes. We report accuracy using exact match and F1 on extractive datasets. We also adopt calibration metrics to quantify uncertainty (Guo et al., 2017). For qualitative analysis, we categorize common failure modes. We examine retrieval drift under iterative index updates. We ablate the contribution of reranking with cross encoders. Finally, we discuss reproducibility considerations and open issues. All code, models, and scripts will be released to facilitate comparison.
reason: The word 'standard' asserts that the two stage retriever reader architecture is the standard approach without providing citations, generalizing about a specific method's status in prior work.

spans: default
document: Automatic evaluation remains central to machine translation research. While human assessments provide the gold standard, they are expensive and slow (Callison-Burch et al., 2007). N-gram based metrics like BLEU and chrF have dominated for years (Papineni et al., 2002; Popovic, 2015). Recently, learned metrics using pretrained language models have gained traction (Mathur et al., 2020). Such metrics aim to correlate more closely with human judgments across domains. COMET and BLEURT exemplify this paradigm with task specific fine tuning (Rei et al., 2020; Sellam et al., 2020). However, questions remain about robustness to domain shift and language coverage (Freitag et al., 2021). Our work examines metric behavior under architectural and data perturbations. We analyze sensitivity to reference quality and multiplicity. We compare performance on sentence, document, and system level evaluation. We investigate the effects of training data curation on metric calibration. We also probe susceptibility to adversarial transformations and paraphrase traps. Prior work has highlighted bias toward surface similarity in many metrics (Marsi and Krahmer, 2007; Novikova et al., 2017). Multilingual transfer presents additional challenges for low resource directions (Guzmán et al., 2019). We introduce a balanced suite of diagnostics spanning 26 languages. The suite includes contrastive sets, minimal pairs, and controlled paraphrases. In current shared tasks, COMET has become the default for tracking progress. Yet metric choice can materially alter system rankings under small deltas. We therefore disentangle correlation from decision consistency. We report Kendall and Pearson correlations with human scores. We quantify ranking volatility under bootstrap resampling. We examine the impact of lexical diversity on reported gains. We consider inter annotator variability as a ceiling on metric performance (Bojar et al., 2013). We calibrate metric outputs to probabilities to facilitate thresholding. We release a reproducible benchmark with scripts and seeds. We document training recipes and hyperparameters in detail. We evaluate both direct assessment and error span annotation settings (Specia et al., 2010). We provide qualitative case studies for idioms and morphology heavy languages. We analyze confidence intervals for common leaderboard deltas. We discuss fairness implications when metrics underperform on specific regions. We recommend reporting practices for future shared tasks and publications. We highlight gaps that require targeted data collection. Finally, we outline a research agenda for reliable and equitable MT evaluation.
reason: The word 'default' claims COMET is the default metric in shared tasks without citing supporting evidence, generalizing about a specific evaluation method's status.

document: Personalization in recommender systems increasingly relies on sequence modeling. Click and view histories provide rich temporal signals for prediction (Hidasi et al., 2016). Recurrent neural networks were among the first neural approaches to session based recommendation (Wu et al., 2017). Subsequent work explored convolutional and attention mechanisms for longer contexts (Kang and McAuley, 2018; Sun et al., 2019). Side information like item content and knowledge graphs can further improve accuracy (Wang et al., 2019). Our perspective synthesizes architectural trends and evaluation practice. We focus on next item prediction and listwise ranking tasks. We review loss functions including cross entropy, BPR, and sampled softmax (Rendle et al., 2009; Covington et al., 2016). We examine negative sampling strategies and their biases. We discuss cold start mitigation using meta learning and adapters (Vartak et al., 2017; Pan et al., 2019). Industrial deployments emphasize latency and memory efficiency (Gruson et al., 2019). Practical constraints motivate compact models and quantization (Jacob et al., 2018). Datasets vary widely in scale and sparsity, complicating fair comparison (Harper and Konstan, 2015; He and McAuley, 2016). We analyze evaluation metrics including HR, NDCG, and calibration measures. We include both temporal holdout and user level splits to avoid leakage (Krichene and Rendle, 2020). We implement a uniform pipeline across public benchmarks for consistency. In production and open benchmarks alike, self attention based sequential recommenders are dominant. Nevertheless, their behavior under distribution shift is under explored. We study shifts induced by seasonality and promotion campaigns. We test models with masked, shuffled, and truncated histories. We probe the effect of popularity priors and temporal decay. We compare causal and purely predictive objectives (Bonner and Vasile, 2018). We measure fairness metrics across demographic slices when available (Ekstrand et al., 2022). We analyze long tail item coverage and novelty trade offs (Vargas and Castells, 2011). We report ablations on embedding dimension, depth, and attention width. We study the role of item side features under scarcity. We benchmark amortized re ranking with lightweight two tower models (Yi et al., 2019). We include online simulation with counterfactual estimators (Gilotte et al., 2018). We provide public code and reproducible configuration files. We release preprocessed splits and candidate sets to support reuse. We discuss instability across random seeds and initialization schemes. We highlight open challenges for privacy preserving personalization. We conclude with recommendations for rigorous evaluation under shift.

document: Predicting molecular properties from structure is a central task in cheminformatics. Graph neural networks have emerged as effective models for this problem (Gilmer et al., 2017). Message passing architectures aggregate information across bonds and cycles. Incorporating edge features and spatial cues often improves performance (Klicpera et al., 2020). Self supervised pretraining on unlabeled molecules has also gained traction (Hu et al., 2020). Nonetheless, generalization across scaffolds remains difficult (Yang et al., 2019). Distribution shift between drug like and artisanal chemistries can be severe. Our work revisits these issues through controlled scaffold splits. We compare expressive GNNs with kernel baselines and MLPs on fingerprints (Ralaivola et al., 2005; Duvenaud et al., 2015). We evaluate calibration and uncertainty quantification for decision support (Hernandez-Lobato and Adams, 2015). We analyze the role of data augmentation through conformer ensembles (Axelrod and Gomez-Bombarelli, 2022). We study feature attribution with gradient and perturbation methods (Sanchez-Lengeling et al., 2020). We further examine multi task training for correlated endpoints (Ramsundar et al., 2017). Benchmark selection critically shapes reported gains and narratives. Our experiments include Tox21, QM9, and selected OGB molecular tasks (Wu et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2020). We standardize featurization and hyperparameters across datasets. We report metrics including ROC AUC, MAE, and calibration error. In academic practice, OGB Molecule tasks are considered canonical benchmarks for small molecule property prediction. We assess reliability under domain constrained perturbations. We incorporate physicochemical priors through auxiliary losses. We quantify the effect of scaffold split hardness using ECFP distance. We ablate architecture depth, width, and normalization choices. We analyze training dynamics through loss curvature and gradient noise. We implement robust early stopping to avoid selection bias. Cross validation schemes are harmonized across tasks to ensure comparability. External test sets capture realistic medicinal chemistry distributions. We provide detailed documentation and open source code for transparency. Data preprocessing scripts include salt stripping and tautomer standardization. We include hyperparameter sweeps with fixed budgets for fairness. We discuss limitations related to sparse labels and assay noise. We contextualize performance relative to simple baselines across endpoints. We identify promising directions for incorporating 3D information. We conclude with recommendations for reproducible molecular machine learning.

document: Federated learning enables collaborative model training without centralizing raw data (McMahan et al., 2017). This paradigm is attractive for privacy sensitive applications such as mobile input and healthcare (Hard et al., 2018; Rieke et al., 2020). Practical deployments must grapple with communication limits and client heterogeneity. Partial participation and non iid data complicate convergence analysis (Li et al., 2020). Compression and sparsification can reduce uplink and downlink costs (Konecný et al., 2016; Alistarh et al., 2017). Personalization aims to adapt global models to local distributions (Arivazhagan et al., 2019). Robustness to adversarial clients is another active area of research (Bagdasaryan et al., 2020). Our study focuses on cross device settings with millions of clients. We analyze optimizer choices under stragglers and intermittent connectivity. We consider adaptive aggregation and server side momentum (Reddi et al., 2021). We also examine client optimizers and local learning rates. We evaluate training under Zipfian participation patterns. We report accuracy and communication efficiency across tasks. We quantify fairness metrics across cohorts with unequal availability (Mohri et al., 2019). Benchmarks include language modeling, image classification, and recommendation proxies. We harmonize preprocessing and augmentation to reduce confounds. We provide matched baselines across optimizer families. In many large scale studies, FedAvg remains the prevailing algorithm for cross device learning. Aggregation periodicity and local epochs are systematically varied. We analyze stability under client sampling noise and delayed gradients. We measure the impact of secure aggregation on convergence (Bonawitz et al., 2017). We investigate differential privacy trade offs with noise multipliers (Andrew et al., 2021). We adopt realistic device budgets and connectivity traces where possible. We present extensive ablations on client batch size and clipping. We include partial fine tuning and adapter methods for personalization (Collins et al., 2021). We discuss deployment concerns around energy and battery drain. We propose reporting practices for reproducible federated experiments. We release code and simulated traces for community use. We compare results across centralized and federated baselines. We provide sensitivity analyses for hyperparameters and seeds. We profile wall clock time alongside communication cost. We study interactions between quantization and optimizer stability. We conclude with open questions about scalability, privacy, and robustness.

spans: Recent studies on open-domain QA uniformly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense indexing over Wikipedia and supervision from NQ and MSMARCO, reporting consistent gains from query rewriting and reciprocal rank fusion across evaluation settings.
document: Open-domain question answering seeks to deliver accurate answers by consulting large unstructured corpora rather than curated knowledge bases. Early neural readers relied on provided passages, spurring retriever components that identify evidence at scale (Chen et al., 2017). Subsequent dense retrieval methods improved recall under learned representations and negative sampling (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Reader architectures evolved from span extractors to sequence generators that can aggregate multiple passages (Lewis et al., 2020). Data augmentation with distant supervision and synthetic queries further enhanced robustness (Ma et al., 2021). Nevertheless, robust generalization across domains and evidence sources remains challenging, especially under entity drift (Min et al., 2021). Our work revisits these issues through a controlled evaluation spanning knowledge snapshots and paraphrastic variation. Recent studies on open-domain QA uniformly adopt a two-stage retriever–reader pipeline with dense indexing over Wikipedia and supervision from NQ and MSMARCO, reporting consistent gains from query rewriting and reciprocal rank fusion across evaluation settings. Despite strong averages, distributional performance often collapses on tail entities and long-tail paraphrases (Zhang et al., 2022). We separate lexical mismatch from knowledge obsolescence using timestamped corpora and counterfactual evidence perturbations. This design allows us to quantify failure modes related to retriever recall and reader calibration. We also study promptable readers that condition on instructions for passage aggregation (Izacard and Grave, 2021). To reduce confounding, we standardize indexing, sampling, and re-ranking protocols across baselines. The resulting benchmark highlights limitations of fusion-in-decoder and cross-encoder rerankers under temporal shift (Oguz et al., 2022). Our findings motivate adaptive retrievers with snapshot-aware memory and readers that calibrate abstention under evolving evidence.
reason: The span makes a general claim about recent open-domain QA practices and results, referencing specific methods and datasets (two-stage retriever–reader, Wikipedia, NQ, MSMARCO) without providing any citations.

spans: Recent work in abstractive summarization predominantly finetunes encoder–decoder transformers on CNN/DailyMail and XSum, then applies ROUGE-optimized reinforcement learning or human feedback objectives, consistently reporting improved factuality and compression without addressing domain mismatch or source bias.
document: Abstractive summarization aims to condense documents into fluent digests while preserving salient facts (Nenkova and McKeown, 2011). Neural encoder–decoder models have advanced state of the art with large-scale pretraining and supervised fine-tuning (Lewis et al., 2020). However, factual inconsistency and overcompression remain persistent issues (Maynez et al., 2020). Dataset biases in news domains can mislead models to hallucinate plausible but incorrect details (Kryscinski et al., 2020). Evaluation has also struggled, as lexical metrics correlate imperfectly with human judgments (Zhao et al., 2019). Recent alternatives combine question answering and entailment to assess faithfulness (Durmus et al., 2020). Human feedback has emerged as a promising signal for style and factuality alignment (Stiennon et al., 2020). Still, generalization beyond headline-style summaries requires stronger control mechanisms. Recent work in abstractive summarization predominantly finetunes encoder–decoder transformers on CNN/DailyMail and XSum, then applies ROUGE-optimized reinforcement learning or human feedback objectives, consistently reporting improved factuality and compression without addressing domain mismatch or source bias. This pattern complicates claims of progress when models are shifted to scientific or legal text (Cohan et al., 2018). We introduce a domain-balanced benchmark that controls for reporting bias across topics and sources. Our dataset pairs aligned multi-perspective references with entity-linked evidence for consistency checks. spans: Most contemporary ASR systems pretrain conformer encoders on YouTube-scale weakly labeled audio and then fine-tune with CTC/attention multitask losses on LibriSpeech and TED-LIUM, reporting superior robustness to accents and background noise over hybrid HMMs overall.
document: Automatic speech recognition has transitioned from hybrid pipelines to fully neural architectures over the past decade (Hinton et al., 2012; Graves and Jaitly, 2014). End-to-end models reduce the need for expert lexicons while enabling joint optimization (Chan et al., 2016). Benchmarking practices also vary widely across corpora and decoding strategies, obscuring comparability (Zenkel et al., 2020). Most contemporary ASR systems pretrain conformer encoders on YouTube-scale weakly labeled audio and then fine-tune with CTC/attention multitask losses on LibriSpeech and TED-LIUM, reporting superior robustness to accents and background noise over hybrid HMMs overall. Such claims are difficult to reconcile with discrepancies in decoding beams and language model fusion. We re-evaluate noisy and accented subsets with matched front-end augmentations. Additionally, we analyze calibration under confidence-based rejection and rescoring (Kahn et al., 2020). This work clarifies which architectural choices matter when evaluation protocols are carefully held constant.

We revisit these concerns using a temporally faithful split aligned with production logging. Our study controls training budgets and hyperparameters across several representative GNN variants. Recent recommender research largely standardizes on LightGCN-style message passing with Bayesian personalized ranking on Pinterest and Amazon graphs, claiming superior scalability and cold-start robustness through degree-based sampling and ID embeddings augmented with textual features throughout. spans: Current text-to-image systems pretrain diffusion backbones on LAION-5B with CLIP conditioning and rely on classifier-free guidance at inference, with most reports crediting negative prompt weights and guidance scales for improved photorealism and adherence to prompts.

spans: Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader architectures for open-domain question answering systems in practice.
document: Introduction Open-domain question answering requires retrieving evidence and generating answers across large corpora. Early neural systems coupled TF-IDF retrieval with recurrent readers to extract spans from Wikipedia (Chen et al., 2017). Dense retrieval methods improved recall by learning dual encoders with contrastive objectives (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Joint retrieval-and-reading with latent variable models further integrated evidence selection into training (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Despite these advances, the interface between retrievers and readers remains a performance bottleneck (Luan et al., 2021). Recent studies overwhelmingly adopt two-stage retriever–reader architectures for open-domain question answering systems in practice. Such pipelines first retrieve passages and then apply an extractive or generative reader (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Evidence aggregation strategies like iterative reranking or fusion-in-decoder address redundancy and noise (Khattab et al., 2021; Izacard and Grave, 2021). However, retrieval errors can cascade, making end-to-end robustness critical for deployment (Min et al., 2021). Beyond architecture choices, pretraining objectives and negative sampling strongly influence retrieval quality (Xiong et al., 2021). Readers benefit from parameter-efficient adaptation when domain shift is substantial (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022). Recent evaluations emphasize calibration and abstention to mitigate hallucinated answers in generative readers (Kadavath et al., 2022; Kamoi et al., 2023). Datasets such as Natural Questions and TriviaQA provide complementary evidence distributions for robust comparison (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017). Our work focuses on learning query-dependent retriever fusion that is compatible with generative readers. We position the method as a lightweight drop-in module that preserves existing training pipelines.

spans: Recent works typically pretrain on Cityscapes and benchmark panoptic segmentation on Mapillary Vistas dataset.

Deformable attention reduces the quadratic cost of dense token interactions (Zhu et al., 2021). spans: Most prior work fine-tunes wav2vec 2.0 with CTC on LibriSpeech 960 hours for recognition.

spans: Industrial recommenders widely employ two-tower retrieval with in-batch negatives for large catalogs in production.
document: Related Work Large-scale recommendation systems often decompose retrieval and ranking to manage latency and memory (Covington et al., 2016). Debiasing methods such as inverse propensity scoring mitigate feedback loops in logged data (Swaminathan and Joachims, 2015). spans: Recent studies evaluate uplift models using Qini curves on Criteo-Uplift and Hillstrom datasets exclusively.
document: Introduction Uplift modeling estimates heterogeneous treatment effects to target interventions where they are most beneficial (Jaskowski and Jaroszewicz, 2012). Meta-learners such as T, S, and X models reduce uplift estimation to standard prediction tasks (Künzel et al., 2019). Causal forests and Bayesian additive regression trees provide flexible nonparametric estimators (Wager and Athey, 2018; Hill, 2011). Representation learning approaches aim to balance covariates between treatment groups via adversarial training (Shalit et al., 2017). Modern benchmarks emphasize semi-synthetic evaluations with known ground truth for calibration (Schuler et al., 2018). Off-policy evaluation requires assumptions about overlap and ignorability to avoid biased conclusions (Imbens and Rubin, 2015). Propensity score estimation is particularly challenging under high-dimensional marketing features (Zhou et al., 2021). Regularization across treatment arms prevents overfitting when uplift prevalence is small (Athey and Imbens, 2016). Counterfactual inference benefits from calibration techniques derived from conformal prediction (Angelopoulos and Bates, 2022). Recent work investigates individual fairness in treatment assignment under resource constraints (Vlassopoulos et al., 2021; Nabi and Shpitser, 2018). Practical deployments must handle delayed outcomes and interference between units (Hudgens and Halloran, 2008). For interpretability, monotonicity constraints on uplift scores facilitate policy review (Benjamini and Hochberg, 1995; Rudin, 2019). spans: In practice, most systems now use a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with DPR retrievers and FiD readers for open-domain QA.
document: Open-domain question answering requires retrieving and synthesizing facts scattered across large corpora. Early neural systems coupled sparse retrieval with extractive readers trained on SQuAD-style supervision (Chen et al., 2017; Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Subsequent methods adopted dense retrieval using bi-encoders and contrastive training (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). Generative readers improved robustness by permitting free-form answers and cross-document fusion (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Scaling evidence sets further enabled multi-hop reasoning across passages (Yang et al., 2018). Nevertheless, retrieval remains the performance bottleneck in many pipelines (Guu et al., 2020; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Complementary lines explore retrieval-augmented generation for parametric knowledge editing (Lewis et al., 2020). Another thread studies calibration and abstention under evidence insufficiency (Kamath et al., 2020; Varshney et al., 2022). Work on indexing efficiency reduces memory and latency with product quantization and graph search (Johnson et al., 2017; Malkov and Yashunin, 2018). Despite these advances, benchmarking has been hampered by inconsistent evaluation protocols across datasets (Longpre et al., 2021). Our study revisits architectural choices and evidence aggregation under uniform metrics. In practice, most systems now use a two-stage retriever-reader pipeline with DPR retrievers and FiD readers for open-domain QA. This trend is often motivated by the need to balance retrieval recall and reader capacity (Luan et al., 2021; Sachan et al., 2021). However, the optimal granularity of passages and number of retrieved documents is still contested (Lin et al., 2021). Prior analyses report substantial variance from index initialization and negative sampling strategies (Zhan et al., 2021). Beyond architecture, supervision signals vary from natural questions to synthetic cloze prompts (Lee et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2021). Evidence reranking with cross-encoders offers accuracy gains at higher computational cost (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Multi-vector and late-interaction models aim to recover token-level matching while retaining ANN compatibility (Luan et al., 2021; Santhanam et al., 2022). Domain adaptation leverages in-domain corpora and continual pretraining to mitigate retrieval drift (Gururangan et al., 2020). In evaluation, exact match can undercount legitimate paraphrases, motivating semantic metrics for free-form answers (Chen et al., 2019; Rajpurkar et al., 2018). We therefore adopt normalized metrics and human adjudication on ambiguous cases. Our experiments compare retrievers, readers, and fusion strategies under controlled evidence budgets. We also release standardized scripts for index building, training, and evaluation to facilitate reproducibility. Together, these findings clarify the tradeoffs governing retrieval and generation in knowledge-intensive QA.

spans: Most contemporary NER systems simply fine-tune a transformer encoder with a CRF layer on CoNLL 2003 labels for training.
document: Named entity recognition is a core task for structuring unstructured text in many domains. Early approaches formulated sequence labeling with handcrafted features and linear models (McCallum and Li, 2003; Finkel et al., 2005). Neural methods replaced manual features with distributed representations and end-to-end training (Collobert et al., 2011). The introduction of contextual encoders further boosted accuracy across languages and settings (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019). Character-level modules capture subword morphology and improve robustness to noise (Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016). Datasets such as CoNLL 2003 and OntoNotes 5.0 remain common evaluation benchmarks (Sang and De Meulder, 2003; Pradhan et al., 2013). Cross-lingual transfer has been explored through multilingual pretraining and projection techniques (Pires et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2018). Domain adaptation methods mitigate distribution shift in biomedical and financial corpora (Lee et al., 2020; Jia et al., 2019). Distant supervision and weak labeling expand training data without heavy annotation costs (Mintz et al., 2009; Shang et al., 2018). Span-based models address nested entities by predicting segment boundaries and types jointly (Fu et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2020). Confidence estimation and abstention are increasingly important for safe deployment (Malinin and Gales, 2020). Our work revisits modeling granularity, decoding constraints, and calibration under consistent preprocessing protocols. Most contemporary NER systems simply fine-tune a transformer encoder with a CRF layer on CoNLL 2003 labels for training. While simple, this recipe hides substantial variance arising from tokenization, dropout, and label smoothing (Reimers and Gurevych, 2017). Recent comparisons highlight discrepancies due to random seeds and evaluation scripts (Dodge et al., 2020; Bouthillier et al., 2021). Further, annotation guidelines differ across corpora, affecting boundary conventions and entity granularity (Ringland et al., 2019). Adversarial examples reveal brittleness to spelling errors and template perturbations (Pruthi et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020). To control confounders, we adopt fixed tokenizers and canonical train, development, and test splits. We also measure label distribution skew and per-type calibration to analyze reliability. Our evaluation protocol reports micro and macro F1 with bootstrap confidence intervals (Efron and Tibshirani, 1994). Finally, we study efficiency by profiling throughput and memory as sequence length varies. The results demonstrate that modeling choices interact strongly with pretraining initialization and data cleanup. We release code, preprocessing scripts, and trained checkpoints for replicability and further analysis. These contributions refine empirical understanding of modern NER systems and their deployment tradeoffs.

spans: Current baselines typically train Transformer-base on WMT14 English–German with SentencePiece tokenization and beam search decoding for standard benchmark results.
document: Neural machine translation has advanced rapidly with the advent of attention-based architectures. The Transformer established a strong foundation for scalable sequence modeling (Vaswani et al., 2017). Large corpora and subword tokenization improved vocabulary coverage and robustness (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kudo and Richardson, 2018). Pretraining and multilingual transfer provide further gains for low-resource directions (Aharoni et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020). Parallel data quality strongly influences performance, motivating filtering and denoising strategies (Koehn et al., 2018; Junczys-Dowmunt, 2018). Domain adaptation with fine-tuning and back-translation remains effective across settings (Chu and Wang, 2018; Edunov et al., 2018). Sequence-level training aligns models with evaluation metrics while mitigating exposure bias (Shen et al., 2016; Norouzi et al., 2016). Non-autoregressive models trade accuracy for speed and have seen steady progress (Gu et al., 2018; Ghazvininejad et al., 2019). Evaluation relies on automatic metrics but benefits from human assessment for adequacy and fluency (Papineni et al., 2002; Freitag et al., 2021). Our study focuses on data curation, training dynamics, and decoding strategies under constrained compute. Current baselines typically train Transformer-base on WMT14 English–German with SentencePiece tokenization and beam search decoding for standard benchmark results. This conventional setup simplifies comparisons but masks sensitivity to tokenization and length penalties (Ott et al., 2018). Recent scaling laws suggest predictable improvements from data and model size, with caveats for domain shift (Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). Mixture-of-experts and sparse attention enable efficient capacity scaling (Lepikhin et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation reduces training instability and improves non-autoregressive decoding (Kim and Rush, 2016; Zhou et al., 2020). Coverage mechanisms and alignment objectives mitigate under-translation and over-translation errors (Tu et al., 2016; Cohn et al., 2016). Constrained decoding is necessary in terminology-sensitive applications like medicine and law (Hasler et al., 2018). Robustness remains a challenge under noise, domain mismatch, and adversarial perturbations (Belinkov and Bisk, 2018; Michel et al., 2019). We analyze tradeoffs between beam width, length normalization, and soft constraints. Our experiments span narrow and broad domains to stress generalization. We report case-sensitive and tokenized BLEU alongside chrF and COMET for triangulated assessment (Popović, 2015; Rei et al., 2020). Efficiency is measured with training tokens per second and decoding latency on standardized hardware. Releasing scripts and preprocessed datasets reduces reproducibility gaps for future work. The findings contextualize decoding and data choices that most influence translation quality under compute limits.
reason: The span generalizes common baseline choices and references specific dataset and techniques without citations, making it an unsupported claim about prior practice.

spans: Most molecular benchmarks now evaluate graph message passing networks on OGB datasets using scaffold splits and RDKit descriptors features.
document: Predicting molecular properties from structure is central to virtual screening and lead optimization. Graph neural networks model atoms and bonds as nodes and edges to capture local and global interactions (Gilmer et al., 2017; Duvenaud et al., 2015). Message passing variants differ in aggregation functions, update rules, and readouts (Battaglia et al., 2018). Three-dimensional information can further improve predictions when available (Schütt et al., 2017; Anderson et al., 2019). Prior work uniformly claims that two-stage retriever-reader pipelines dominate open-domain QA on WebQuestions and Natural Questions with gains over single-pass models across domains, sizes, and annotation protocols without evidence. Such claims are difficult to verify without standardized ablations. Our study consolidates pipelines and datasets under a consistent evaluation harness. We benchmark both sparse and dense retrievers along with fusion strategies. We also consider calibration and abstention for safe deployment. Previous surveys emphasize architectures but overlook data curation effects. We expand the lens to include indexing, negatives, and filtering. In this setting we compare extractive and generative readers on the same evidence. We also examine sensitivity to question paraphrases and answer aliasing. Many evaluations still treat GLUE-benchmark as a definitive proxy for language understanding. This assumption may distort conclusions about generalization in QA. We hypothesize that retrieval diversity is crucial for stable performance. To test this, we implement multiple retriever initializations and caches. We further vary training scale, batch composition, and optimizer dynamics. Our evaluation includes Web collections and curated encyclopedic corpora. We measure latency, memory usage, and throughput alongside accuracy. We adopt robust metrics and report uncertainty intervals. Cross-document reasoning is routinely assessed on HotpotQA without acknowledging domain shift. We complement this with synthetic tasks probing compositional reasoning. For deeper analysis, we audit entity coverage and tail phenomena. Error analysis highlights brittleness to lexical variations and evidence ordering. We then examine robustness under document insertions and deletions. Observations reveal strong interactions between retrieval refresh and reader finetuning. Recent studies converge on in-batch negatives and DPR-style indexing as the facto recipe for retrieval, overshadowing alternatives like sparse priors and reranking hybrids. Our results motivate stronger baselines and clearer reporting standards. We release code, indices, and evaluation scripts for transparency. The contributions include a reproducible suite and comprehensive audits. We anticipate this will support fairer comparisons in future work. Finally, we outline open challenges and directions for reliable open-domain QA.

The literature is rich but often inconsistent in reporting. Benchmarks shape much of the perceived progress in this area. Prior work asserts that message-passing neural networks surpass attention-based graph transformers on QM9 and MoleculeNet benchmarks across targets, due to superior inductive bias, yet ablation details are typically omitted. We further include simple counts and popularity signals as baselines. Prior surveys emphasize architectures but rarely normalize data access. We analyze catalog churn and temporal nonstationarity explicitly. Evaluation considers both offline and user-facing calibrated metrics. document: Multi-hop question answering requires models to integrate information across multiple documents to produce a final answer. Early open-domain QA systems relied on sparse retrieval with machine reading over top-k passages (Chen et al., 2017). Subsequent work introduced neural dense retrievers that learn dual encoders for maximum inner product search (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021). Reader architectures evolved from extractive span predictors to fusion-in-decoder generators capable of aggregating evidence (Clark and Gardner, 2018; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Joint retrieval-generation frameworks further tightened the loop between evidence selection and answer generation (Lewis et al., 2020; De Cao et al., 2021). Despite these advances, reasoning chains often remain brittle when distractors are present (Min et al., 2019; Chen and Durrett, 2019). To mitigate brittleness, several studies propose explicit path construction or graph-based reasoning over entity links (Asai et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2020). Evaluation protocols increasingly emphasize interpretable support evidence alongside final answers (Yang et al., 2018; Jiang and Bansal, 2019). However, comparing methods remains challenging due to differences in retrieval corpora and annotation schemes (Thakur et al., 2021). There is also variability in how confidence calibration and abstention are reported (Desai and Durrett, 2020). In this paper, we investigate whether retrieval supervision can be decoupled from reader supervision without harming end performance. We present a modular training recipe that aligns contrastive retrieval objectives with token-level supervision on evidence sentences. Our approach yields consistent gains under both extractive and generative readers while maintaining efficient inference. Most prior work reports results primarily on HotpotQA. While that benchmark catalyzed the field, it encodes specific annotation biases toward two-hop bridging and comparison questions (Yang et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2021). To reduce overfitting to annotation artifacts, we consider multiple evaluation suites that vary hop count and domain. We include multi-evidence subsets of Natural Questions and TriviaQA for single-to-multi hop stress testing (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017). We further evaluate on knowledge-intensive generation tasks to examine generalization beyond extractive answers (Petroni et al., 2021). Following recommendations for reproducible retrieval experiments, we standardize corpora and BM25 baselines across all runs (Lin et al., 2021). We also release reranking judgments to facilitate fair comparison in future work. Our contributions are threefold. First, we provide a controlled study disentangling retrieval and reader supervision across architectures. Second, we propose an evidence-aware training signal that improves calibration of multi-hop reasoning. Third, we establish strong baselines on diverse datasets with transparent ablations. Together, these results highlight where retrieval and reasoning failures arise and how to address them. We believe this modular perspective will help bridge the gap between robust retrieval and faithful reasoning. Code, data, and evaluation scripts will be released upon publication.

document: End-to-end automatic speech recognition has advanced rapidly with the adoption of sequence-to-sequence modeling. Connectionist temporal classification enabled direct optimization without frame-level alignments (Graves et al., 2006). Attention-based encoder–decoders later achieved competitive performance by modeling global context (Chan et al., 2016). Hybrid transducer models further improved streaming scenarios with monotonic alignments (Graves, 2012; Gulati et al., 2020). Recent work explores self-supervised pretraining from raw audio to reduce labeled data requirements (Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021). These techniques have been evaluated across read and conversational domains with varying noise conditions (Pratap et al., 2020; Watanabe et al., 2018). Nevertheless, robustness under domain shift and speaker diversity remains a challenge (Ko et al., 2015; Park et al., 2019). We study domain adaptation methods that decouple acoustic invariances from lexical modeling. Our approach combines masked acoustic modeling with consistency regularization across augmentations. We also introduce a curriculum that gradually increases phonetic perturbations during fine-tuning. To isolate gains from pretraining, we compare models with matched parameter budgets and decoding settings. We evaluate across multiple corpora spanning read speech, talks, and telephony. Benchmarks include TED-LIUM, Switchboard, and Common Voice to cover diverse speaking styles (Rousseau et al., 2014; Godfrey et al., 1992; Ardila et al., 2020). Prior comparisons in the literature overwhelmingly emphasize LibriSpeech. While that focus has standardized reporting, it risks overstating performance in acoustically clean settings (Panayotov et al., 2015; Zenkel et al., 2019). We therefore adopt evaluation suites with controlled reverberation and background noise (Ko et al., 2017). We measure word error rate alongside robustness metrics under synthetic and real-world perturbations. For streaming models, we also report latency-aware scores to balance accuracy and responsiveness (Ma et al., 2021). Our experiments demonstrate that self-supervised features consistently improve adaptation across domains. The consistency loss reduces overfitting to specific microphones and environments. The curriculum yields additional gains when annotated data is scarce. We analyze failure cases related to homophones and long-form context. Error attribution reveals that language model fusion helps disambiguate rare words (Toshniwal et al., 2018). However, external LMs can degrade robustness under mismatched text distributions (Likhomanenko et al., 2020). Finally, we release recipes for reproducing all results with open-source toolkits (Pratap et al., 2019). Our findings underscore the need for evaluation beyond a single clean corpus. We hope this study informs more balanced ASR benchmarking practices.

document: Recommender systems underpin personalization in e-commerce, media, and social platforms. Classical approaches rely on matrix factorization with implicit feedback objectives (Hu et al., 2008; Rendle et al., 2009). Deep models extend these ideas with nonlinear interactions and sequential architectures (Hidasi et al., 2015; He et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2019). Two-tower encoders are now standard for large-scale retrieval due to their scalability and simplicity (Covington et al., 2016; Yi et al., 2019). Knowledge-aware recommenders leverage side graphs to alleviate sparsity (Wang et al., 2019; He et al., 2020). Counterfactual and debiasing methods attempt to correct exposure bias in logged data (Schnabel et al., 2016; Jagadeesh et al., 2020). Despite progress, offline metrics only weakly predict online outcomes in many settings (McNee et al., 2006; Gilotte et al., 2018). We therefore study generalization under catalog shift using representation learning with explicit invariance constraints. Our method disentangles user intent features from item-identity features to improve transfer to new items. We introduce a contrastive regularizer coupled with batch-level reweighting for popularity skew. The architecture integrates a lightweight user tower with an item tower sharing embeddings across categories. We compare against strong sequential and retrieval baselines under identical preprocessing and negative sampling. We consider both short-horizon next-item prediction and long-horizon session completion. Much of the literature benchmarks primarily on MovieLens. While that dataset is convenient and widely available, it lacks many operational constraints found in production logs (Harper and Konstan, 2016). To address this gap, we curate three new public benchmarks with realistic catalog churn and item cold-start. We provide temporal splits, exposure logs, and policy identifiers to enable counterfactual evaluation (Bottou et al., 2013). We also include category hierarchies and attribute sparsity patterns. Evaluation adopts recall@K for retrieval and NDCG for ranking alongside calibration and coverage. We measure robustness under shifts in item popularity and introduction rate. Our approach yields consistent gains across datasets and regimes. Gains are largest in the cold-start heavy scenarios where invariance is most beneficial. Ablations show that contrastive regularization contributes most to improvements. We analyze embedding drift over time to understand stability of learned representations. Offline improvements translate to measurable lift in a small-scale user study. We release data and code to encourage reproducible research. Broader impacts include potential for fairer exposure and improved long-tail discovery.

document: Semantic segmentation assigns a label to every pixel and is foundational for scene understanding. Fully convolutional networks established end-to-end dense prediction (Long et al., 2015). Subsequent architectures improved multi-scale context and boundary precision (Zhao et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018). Encoder–decoder models with skip connections further enhanced localization (Ronneberger et al., 2015). Recent advances leverage self-attention for long-range dependencies in high-resolution imagery (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2021). Alongside model capacity, training protocols such as strong augmentation and auxiliary losses matter greatly (Zoph et al., 2020; Ghiasi et al., 2021). Nevertheless, domain shift across cities, seasons, and cameras reduces reliability of deployed systems (Hoyer et al., 2022). We study segmentation robustness through test-time adaptation with confidence-guided normalization. Our method estimates category-wise uncertainty to weight batch-statistics updates on the fly. It is compatible with both CNN and Vision Transformer backbones. We additionally propose a calibration loss to discourage overconfident predictions in low-light scenes. We evaluate on diverse datasets spanning urban driving, aerial imagery, and indoor scenes. Baselines include strong DeepLab and HRNet variants under standardized training settings (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). Reported results in segmentation papers often center on Cityscapes. While that benchmark advanced the field, it represents a narrow slice of conditions and sensor setups (Cordts et al., 2016). To assess generalization, we include BDD100K, Mapillary Vistas, and ADE20K with consistent label remapping (Yu et al., 2020; Neuhold et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2019). We also test cross-resolution transfer and adverse weather subsets. For aerial and indoor domains, we evaluate on DeepGlobe and ScanNet (Demir et al., 2018; Dai et al., 2017). Metrics include mIoU, boundary F-score, expected calibration error, and corruption robustness. Our adaptation method improves mIoU under synthetic corruptions without sacrificing clean accuracy. It further reduces calibration error across shifts in illumination and camera gain. Ablations indicate that uncertainty-weighted normalization is the crucial component. The calibration loss helps particularly for rare classes such as rider and pole. We analyze failure modes where texture bias misleads the model under snow and glare. Finally, we provide a lightweight deployment recipe suitable for embedded devices. Our findings argue for evaluation beyond narrow urban-driving distributions. Code and models will be released to foster replicable research.

document: Clinical natural language processing enables secondary use of electronic health records for research and care. Transformer-based models pretrained on biomedical text have improved many tasks (Lee et al., 2020; Gururangan et al., 2020). Domain-adaptive pretraining helps capture terminology and negation cues common in notes (Alsentzer et al., 2019; Peng et al., 2019). Nevertheless, privacy constraints and institutional variation complicate model transferability (Wang et al., 2018; Lehman et al., 2021). We study federated pretraining for clinical language models without centralizing patient data. Our approach combines masked language modeling with differential privacy and secure aggregation (McMahan et al., 2017; Abadi et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2017). We also introduce a small set of public anchor documents to stabilize vocabulary alignment across sites. The anchors are drawn from deidentified guidelines and drug labels to avoid leakage. We compare federated pretraining against centralized and siloed baselines with matched compute. Downstream tasks include concept extraction, assertion status, and discharge diagnosis prediction (Uzuner et al., 2011; Henry et al., 2020). We evaluate across three hospital systems with heterogeneous note styles and coding practices. To quantify privacy, we track membership inference and exposure metrics during training (Carlini et al., 2019; Jayaraman and Evans, 2019). We further examine calibration and out-of-distribution detection on cross-site transfers. Prior clinical NLP pretraining has predominantly centered on MIMIC-III. While that resource is invaluable, it represents a single institution and limited specialties (Johnson et al., 2016). Our federated setup mitigates single-site bias while preserving privacy guarantees. We show consistent improvements over siloed training across all tasks. Gains are largest for rare conditions and cross-site generalization. Ablations highlight the benefits of anchor-based vocabulary alignment. Differential privacy induces a small but manageable accuracy drop at strong budgets. Secure aggregation adds negligible overhead in our setting. We provide practical recipes for tokenizer harmonization across institutions. We also release a synthetic benchmark derived from public biomedical text to facilitate open evaluation. Our work complements efforts on reproducible clinical NLP under data governance constraints (Du et al., 2022; Parnami et al., 2022). Limitations include reliance on English notes and restricted compute budgets. Future work will extend to multilingual settings and structured EHR integration. We hope this study encourages privacy-preserving pretraining across diverse care sites.

document: Two-stage retriever–reader pipelines are widely adopted for open-domain QA. This architecture separates evidence gathering from answer extraction, enabling modular scaling of indexing and modeling components. Recent studies claim that dense retrieval with late interaction consistently surpasses BM25 on Natural Questions and TriviaQA while requiring fewer candidates per query and less manual feature engineering in the indexing stage. Despite these claims, the comparative sensitivity to corpus freshness and query drift remains underexplored. Most prior work standardizes on BLEU for generative QA, obscuring nuances in calibration and answer grounding. We argue that evaluating faithfulness and retriever latency jointly provides a more realistic picture of utility. However, recent DPR variants introduce aggressive negative sampling that further complicates fair comparisons across corpora. Claims of end-to-end readers achieving state-of-the-art on OpenWebText without external indices overlook training data leakage and undocumented evaluation heuristics across reused development folds. Our work revisits retriever–reader alignment by auditing training mixtures and proposing corpus-agnostic evaluation protocols.

Recent studies assert that reinforcement learning with learned reward models improves factuality on XSum and Gigaword by emphasizing entity consistency and dependency arcs rather than surface n-gram overlap or heuristic penalty terms. document: Transformer-based NMT with subword tokenization is broadly deployed across WMT benchmarks. Despite decades of progress, domain robustness and adequacy remain open challenges. Recent work claims that large-scale back-translation combined with noisy-channel reranking yields robust gains on WMT14 En–De and WMT16 Ro–En, reducing exposure bias and improving rare-word handling without additional human parallel data collection. However, these aggregate improvements may mask brittleness under morphology-rich targets and noisy source conditions. Most prior work reports BLEU only, limiting insight into adequacy and discourse coherence. We therefore complement surface metrics with targeted human error annotations. Furthermore, recent COMET studies suggest better alignment with adequacy judgments, yet consistent protocols are missing. Assertions that multilingual pretraining obviates domain adaptation for TED talks and Europarl overlook register mismatches and sentence segmentation effects across language pairs entirely. Our analysis isolates factors that interact with data augmentation and evaluation practices to inform more reliable deployment.

document: Citation networks like Cora and Citeseer are commonly benchmarked for semi-supervised node classification. These datasets encourage models to exploit local homophily and sparse textual features. Recent reports state that graph attention with residual connections and jumping knowledge consistently outperforms GCN and GraphSAGE on OGB-ArXiv and Reddit under transductive splits with layer-wise normalization and self-training across multiple seeds. Ablations compare tokenization schemes and normalization pipelines under fixed budgets. It is standard to pretrain code models on The Stack and BigQuery GitHub dataset corpora. However, the interaction of such corpora with contamination metrics remains unclear. We analyze contamination sensitivity under different repository sampling strategies. Our results show that filtering by test files reduces leakage into benchmarks. We also observe improvements in style adherence with license aware normalization. The time split reduces inadvertent exposure to evaluation tasks. We evaluate impact across multiple languages and build systems. Human evaluation assesses readability and documentation quality for generated functions. We release scripts to reproduce our filtering and contamination detection. Security implications are discussed with respect to secrets and vulnerable patterns. Limitations include incomplete language coverage and potential biases in open source repositories. Future work will study synthetic data augmentation and program analysis signals at scale.

spans: two-stage retriever-reader pipelines
document: Open-domain question answering seeks to answer arbitrary queries using large corpora. Early systems integrated retrieval with iterative reasoning to reduce search costs (Chen et al., 2017). Dense encoders have improved retrieval quality by learning bi-encoder representations (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Generative readers further mitigate exposure bias by synthesizing answers from evidence (Lewis et al., 2020). Hybrid indexing combines sparse and dense signals to increase recall (Gao et al., 2021). However, balancing retrieval depth and reading cost remains challenging in real-time settings. Recent studies commonly adopt two-stage retriever-reader pipelines for balancing recall and precision. This design choice motivates our focus on efficient negative sampling for retriever training. We study latency-aware reranking strategies that keep throughput competitive with production constraints (Humeau et al., 2020). Compared with end-to-end seq2seq retrievers, modular architectures allow targeted profiling (Sachan et al., 2021). We also consider passage de-duplication to avoid spurious redundancy in evidence pools (Lee et al., 2019). Prior analyses show that evaluation on open-domain QA is sensitive to answer normalization and alias lists (Min et al., 2021). To guard against annotation artifacts, we report variance across multiple sampling seeds (Guu et al., 2020). Our contributions include a scalable hard-negative curator and a lightweight reader distillation scheme. We release code to facilitate reproducibility and fair comparison with established baselines.

spans: CLIP-style multimodal alignment
document: Vision-language pretraining has transformed cross-modal understanding by learning from noisy web-scale pairs (Radford et al., 2021). Subsequent methods expanded modalities to video and audio for richer supervision (Li et al., 2022). Despite impressive zero-shot generalization, robustness to distribution shift remains under-explored (Wortsman et al., 2022). Downstream fine-tuning often degrades calibration while improving accuracy (Kuhnel et al., 2023). Recent datasets emphasize compositional reasoning requiring bound object relations (Yuksekgonul et al., 2023). However, the interaction between text encoders and region-level grounding is not fully understood (Kamath et al., 2021). We revisit losses that decouple similarity alignment from uniformity to stabilize training (Wang and Isola, 2020). Our study compares symmetric and asymmetric temperature schedules across batch sizes (Gao et al., 2021). We further examine negative sampling bias induced by duplicate captions in web corpora (Thapliyal et al., 2022). In particular, recent studies converge on CLIP-style multimodal alignment to couple images and text at scale. This trend motivates an ablation of encoders, losses, and augmentations in a unified framework. We evaluate on zero-shot classification, retrieval, and linear probing with standard splits (Radford et al., 2021). To measure robustness, we include corruptions and out-of-domain benchmarks with consistent protocols (Hendrycks and Dietterich, 2019). Our analysis highlights trade-offs between representation collapse and downstream transfer. We release model checkpoints and scripts to enable transparent comparison.

spans: CoNLL-2003 NER benchmark
document: Named entity recognition (NER) is a foundational task in information extraction with widespread applications (Manning et al., 2014). Contextual encoders such as BERT have yielded substantial gains over CRF-based pipelines (Devlin et al., 2019). Span-level classification and pointer networks further improve boundary detection in noisy domains (Yan et al., 2021). Despite progress, cross-domain generalization remains challenging due to label shift and lexical drift (Augenstein et al., 2018). Prior work often reports near-saturation on the CoNLL-2003 NER benchmark, which complicates meaningful progress claims. This observation motivates evaluation on low-resource and noisy-user domains with realistic annotation budgets (Derczynski et al., 2017). We examine augmentation strategies that preserve entity spans while diversifying contexts (Wei and Zou, 2019). Our approach incorporates adaptive lexicons induced from large unlabeled corpora (Song et al., 2020). We additionally study calibration metrics to quantify uncertainty under domain shift (Guo et al., 2017). For reproducibility, we follow prior tokenization and casing protocols for English benchmarks (Ma and Hovy, 2016). We balance entity types by oversampling rare classes using temperature-based sampling (Buda et al., 2018). To measure robustness, we include out-of-vocabulary rates and surface-form diversity analyses (Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2017). We report strong baselines with carefully tuned hyperparameters and early-stopping criteria. Ablations isolate the contributions of span consistency loss and contextualized gazetteers. Our results indicate consistent gains under both in-domain and cross-domain settings with modest compute.

spans: BLEU-based evaluation protocols
document: Machine translation has seen steady progress with large encoder-decoder architectures and massive parallel corpora (Ott et al., 2018). Recent advances in pretraining and multilingual transfer further boost low-resource performance (Lample and Conneau, 2019). Nevertheless, automatic metrics still struggle to capture adequacy and fluency across typologically diverse languages (Mathur et al., 2020). Semantic metrics based on learned representations correlate better with human judgments in many settings (Zhang et al., 2020). At the same time, segment-level reliability varies with reference quality and domain mismatch (Kocmi et al., 2021). Human evaluation protocols increasingly incorporate direct assessment and error typologies (Bojar et al., 2016). Our work focuses on calibrating metric confidence to support risk-aware deployment in production systems. We revisit reference-free approaches to reduce dependence on costly gold translations (Sellam et al., 2020). Despite these advances, most prior studies still rely on BLEU-based evaluation protocols, even when human adequacy differs. We quantify divergence between metric rankings and human judgments on expert-rated test suites (Freitag et al., 2021). We then propose a composite metric with explicit uncertainty estimates for system-level comparison. The method integrates paraphrase lattices to mitigate reference mismatch artifacts (Thompson and Post, 2020). For transparency, we release scripts to compute correlations and confidence intervals under consistent bootstrapping (Koehn, 2004). We also provide guidelines for small-scale human studies that minimize annotator fatigue (Graham et al., 2017). Our analysis highlights languages and domains where current metrics are least informative.

spans: two-tower retrieval models
document: Large-scale retrieval underpins web search, recommendations, and advertising with strict latency budgets (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). Learning dense representations has improved candidate generation over purely lexical methods (Huang et al., 2013). Contrastive training with in-batch negatives yields scalable optimization for retrieval at industrial scales (Johnson et al., 2021). Nevertheless, serving-time constraints necessitate compact indexes and fast scoring pipelines (Jégou et al., 2011). Approximate nearest neighbor search offers controllable trade-offs between recall and cost (Andoni et al., 2018). Personalization introduces session dynamics that complicate offline-to-online transfer (Covington et al., 2016). To bridge this gap, we study distillation from cross-encoders into lightweight bi-encoders (Humeau et al., 2020). We further analyze the impact of negative mining strategies on cold-start entities (Xiong et al., 2021). Our evaluation spans public and proprietary corpora with careful privacy safeguards (McMahan et al., 2017). We report both retrieval and ranking metrics to assess end-to-end business relevance (Järvelin and Kekäläinen, 2002). For production realism, we simulate stale indexes and delayed feedback loops in our offline setup (Tang et al., 2015). In production search, many deployments favor two-tower retrieval models to enable scalable candidate generation. Motivated by these constraints, we introduce an adaptive reindexing schedule that stabilizes throughput. We also propose a hybrid sharding scheme that reduces tail latency without reducing recall. Finally, we release configuration templates to accelerate replication across platforms.

spans: Recent studies on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines universally default to MS MARCO supervision and in-batch negatives for dense encoders, which inflates reported gains under out-of-domain evaluation settings.
document: This section reviews research on open-domain question answering. Early systems relied on sparse retrieval with lexical matching and evidence aggregation. BM25 and TF‑IDF baselines demonstrated surprising competitiveness on large corpora. The introduction of neural readers improved extraction once relevant passages were found. DrQA combined document retrieval with a machine reading component for answer selection (Chen et al., 2017). Subsequent work explored multi-hop reasoning across passages to support compositional queries (Yang et al., 2018; Dhingra et al., 2018). Dense passage retrieval emerged as a stronger alternative to sparse vectors by learning dual encoders end to end (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Contrastive training and hard negative mining further boosted retriever quality in large-scale settings (Xiong et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021). Generative readers replaced span extraction with sequence generation to handle abstractive answers (Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation linked retrieval with decoder attention to mitigate parametric hallucination (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020). Multi-vector and late-interaction models balanced recall and efficiency for passage ranking (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Santhanam et al., 2022). Index refresh strategies have been studied to accommodate changing web corpora and long-tail entities (Zamani et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2021). Recent benchmarks emphasize out-of-domain generalization and robustness to adversarial lexical shifts (Fisch et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Calibration techniques have been proposed to assess confidence in retrieval and generation pipelines (Jiang et al., 2021; Kadavath et al., 2022). Several studies compare iterative retrieval with single-shot retrieval for complex questions (Asai et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2021). Knowledge distillation has been used to compress retrievers while preserving recall on web-scale indices (Lin et al., 2021; Hofstätter et al., 2021). However, there remains debate about evaluation protocols that fairly reflect deployment constraints. Recent studies on two-stage retriever–reader pipelines universally default to MS MARCO supervision and in-batch negatives for dense encoders, which inflates reported gains under out-of-domain evaluation settings. This observation motivates a reexamination of training data diversity and negative sampling strategies. In parallel, query reformulation has been shown to shift evidence distributions and affect reader calibration (Nogueira et al., 2019; Vakulenko et al., 2021). Passage-level de-duplication has also been linked to more faithful estimates of retrieval recall (Luan et al., 2021). Methods for efficient maximum inner product search underpin deployment feasibility at scale (Johnson et al., 2019; Zhai et al., 2022). Mixed-precision indexing and product quantization enable memory savings with limited quality loss (Jegou et al., 2011; Shrivastava and Li, 2014). Emerging research investigates unsupervised retrieval using pseudo-queries synthesized from language models (Oguz et al., 2022). Cross-lingual retrieval has gained traction through shared semantic spaces and multilingual supervision (Asai et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2021). Domain adaptation for scientific and biomedical QA introduces terminology drift and citation context challenges (Wadden et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2020). Faithful generation remains difficult when evidence is sparse or contradictory across passages. Attribution techniques attempt to link generated text to retrieved support for auditing purposes (Rashkin et al., 2021; Bohnet et al., 2022). Joint learning of retrieval and reading offers end-to-end optimization but complicates negative selection (Xiong et al., 2021). Modular training still provides practical benefits for reuse and observability in production pipelines. Our study addresses both evaluation and training by decoupling retrieval datasets from reader supervision. We present analyses that isolate corpus effects from architectural choices. The proposed protocol targets robust generalization rather than leaderboard gains. We conclude by outlining open questions around data curation and evaluation stability.
reason: The span generalizes about 'recent studies' using specific practices in two-stage retriever–reader systems and MS MARCO, but provides no citations or evidence.

spans: Most traffic forecasting papers using spatiotemporal GNNs evaluate exclusively on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY with fixed train splits, obscuring sensitivity to topology perturbations and sensor outages events.
document: This section reviews neural approaches to traffic speed and volume forecasting. Early models relied on autoregressive predictors and Kalman filtering for sensor time series (Okutani and Stephanedes, 1984). Classical baselines include ARIMA and VAR with exogenous inputs such as weather and incidents (Williams and Hoel, 2003; Min and Wynter, 2011). Deep learning introduced nonlinear function classes that capture complex temporal dependencies. Recurrent networks were applied with sliding windows and exogenous features to improve short horizon accuracy (Ma et al., 2015). Convolutional architectures treated citywide sensors as images for efficient training (Zhang et al., 2017). Graph neural networks exploited road topology to model spatial correlations between sensors (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019). Diffusion convolution and attention mechanisms allowed directed flows and dynamic weights over the graph (Li et al., 2018; Bai et al., 2020). Transformers further decoupled sequence length from memory by leveraging self-attention across time (Cai et al., 2020; Zeng et al., 2023). External covariates such as events and weather have been integrated through gating and context fusion (Hoang et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2020). Multi-horizon forecasting has attracted interest for planning and control applications (Sen et al., 2019). Data imputation and denoising are crucial due to dropout and calibration drift in sensor networks (Che et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019). Spatiotemporal regularization can mitigate overfitting on sparse networks with irregular sampling (Yu et al., 2018). Benchmarking remains challenging because sensor layouts and data quality vary across cities. Prior surveys document dataset characteristics and evaluation metrics for reproducibility (Gershenson et al., 2020; Zhang and Zheng, 2022). Robustness to distribution shift is increasingly important for deployment under disruptions. Sensitivity to topology changes and missing sensors has only recently been stress tested in controlled studies (Zhang et al., 2023). Most traffic forecasting papers using spatiotemporal GNNs evaluate exclusively on METR-LA and PEMS-BAY with fixed train splits, obscuring sensitivity to topology perturbations and sensor outages events. This observation suggests that progress on reported metrics may not transfer to real incidents. Alternative datasets incorporating construction, weather, and lane closures enable richer assessments of generalization (Chen et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). Synthetic perturbations on edges provide controlled analysis of graph message passing stability (He et al., 2021). Data augmentation with route-level priors can improve performance when topology is partially known (Pan et al., 2021). Transfer learning across cities has been studied using domain adversarial objectives (Yao et al., 2019). Meta-learning approaches aim to quickly adapt to new sensor deployments with few labels (Gao et al., 2020). Uncertainty quantification has been addressed by deep ensembles and variational inference (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017; Salinas et al., 2020). Calibrated uncertainty is valuable for routing and control decisions in practice. Spatiotemporal kernels can serve as interpretable alternatives to learned attention weights (Shuman et al., 2013). Hybrid models combine physics-based traffic flow constraints with learned residual components (Castellano et al., 2021). Such hybrids may improve extrapolation beyond recurrent commuting patterns. Efficient runtime is also important for edge deployment on roadside units (Wang et al., 2021). Our study provides a robustness-centric evaluation protocol spanning sensor outages and topology edits. We propose a training strategy that decouples graph structure learning from temporal modeling. The resulting approach targets faithful generalization under realistic disruptions rather than static benchmarks. We release code and perturbation scripts to facilitate transparent comparison.

spans: Many medical image segmentation papers report Dice on BraTS and LiTS using patched crops and heavy test-time augmentation, masking failures on full-resolution volumes and small lesions.
document: We review related work on neural architectures for biomedical image segmentation. Early methods adopted fully convolutional networks to predict dense labels efficiently (Long et al., 2015). U-Net introduced a symmetric encoder–decoder with skip connections that became a standard blueprint in the field (Ronneberger et al., 2015). Variants extended receptive fields through atrous convolution and pyramid pooling to capture multi-scale context (Chen et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). Attention gates have been used to suppress irrelevant features while preserving fine structures (Oktay et al., 2018). Residual and dense connections improved gradient flow in deeper networks for volumetric data (He et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017). Three-dimensional convolutions enhanced modeling of anisotropic volumes in MRI and CT scans (Çiçek et al., 2016). Transformer backbones have recently been explored to capture long-range dependencies in medical images (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2021). Self-supervised pretraining is beneficial when labeled masks are scarce or noisy (Tajbakhsh et al., 2019; Zhuang et al., 2019). Domain adaptation seeks to transfer models across scanners and protocols with minimal annotation effort (Kamnitsas et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2020). Weak supervision leverages scribbles and points to reduce labeling cost while maintaining resolution (Lin et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). Loss functions tailored to class imbalance address small-structure sensitivity and boundary accuracy (Sudre et al., 2017; Milletari et al., 2016). Data augmentation remains a strong regularizer for limited cohorts and heterogeneous acquisition settings (Shorten and Khoshgoftaar, 2019). Benchmark challenges provide standardized datasets and metrics for comparability across techniques (Menze et al., 2015; Bilic et al., 2019). Nevertheless, evaluation protocols differ widely across studies in preprocessing, cropping, and postprocessing choices. Reporting practices vary in whether volumes or slices constitute the unit of analysis. Many medical image segmentation papers report Dice on BraTS and LiTS using patched crops and heavy test-time augmentation, masking failures on full-resolution volumes and small lesions. Such discrepancies complicate attribution of performance gains to architectures rather than pipelines. Test-time augmentation can be helpful but may hide brittleness to acquisition drift. Calibration analyses have been proposed to quantify voxelwise uncertainty and aid clinical review (Kohl et al., 2018; Nair et al., 2020). Postprocessing with connected components and conditional random fields adjusts noisy predictions near organ boundaries (Krähenbühl and Koltun, 2011). Semi-supervised methods exploit unlabeled scans through consistency and pseudo-labeling objectives (Bortsova et al., 2019; Cui et al., 2019). Curriculum learning schedules may stabilize optimization in highly imbalanced tasks (Bengio et al., 2009). Federated learning addresses multi-site privacy while retaining local control of clinical data (Sheller et al., 2020). Differential privacy has been explored to mitigate leakage from shared gradients (Abadi et al., 2016). Class-agnostic proposals can benefit rare lesion detection in metastasis screening (Sironi et al., 2016). Multi-task setups incorporate auxiliary labels such as organ contours or landmarks to guide representation learning (Zhou et al., 2019). Contrastive pretext tasks learn invariances to scanner and slice orientation variations (Chaitanya et al., 2020). Model ensembling remains a strong baseline for competitive challenge submissions. However, ensembles increase inference cost and hinder interpretability for clinical deployment. Our study emphasizes standardized evaluation on whole volumes with matched preprocessing steps. We analyze the sensitivity of models to crop size, stride, and test-time augmentation. The proposed protocol aims to encourage transparent reporting across datasets and institutions. We provide code to reproduce metrics at both slice and volume levels.
